The barrage however fell short but it caused the Italians to drop their torpedoes early. Also one of the enemy was possibly shot down.

This attack scattered the convoy and it took some time to reform. At 1945 B/23 about seven bombers appeared from ahead at a height of about 14000 feet to attack the convoy from the port side. The convoy altered 40° to port together and the escort opened up a controlled fire with some hesitation as the Italian aircraft looked a lot like Beaufighters. The bombing was extremely accurate. Several bombs fell near HMS Edinburgh which was leading the port column, and a near miss abreast a boiler room disabled HMS Firedrake which had been sweeping ahead of the convoy. She could no longer steam so Rear-Admiral Syfret ordered her back to Gibraltar in tow of HMS Eridge. They had an anxious passage, being shadowed by aircraft continuously during daylight hours, but were not again attacked. On the 25th HMS Firedrake managed to lit one boiler so the tow was slipped. Both destroyers entered Gibraltar harbour on the 27th.

Soon after leaving the Skerki Channel in the evening of the 23th the convoy hauled up to the north-east towards the coast of Sicily. This was to lessen the danger of mines. The Italians did not shadow the convoy after the attack at 1945 hours and missed this alteration of course which they clearly did not expect. Around 2100 hours, as it was getting dark, enemy aircraft were seen searching along its old line of advance. During the evening the convoy sighted flares several times about 20 miles to the south.
Continued passage to the east and enemy attacks, 24 July 1941

Between 0250 and 0315 hours the convoy was however attacked by the Italian MAS boats MAS 532 and MAS 533. The managed to torpedo and damaged the Sydney Star. HMAS Nestor went alongside and took off almost 500 soldiers. Sydney Star was however able to continue her passage as staggler escorted initially by HMAS Nestor. Admiral Syfret however sent back HMS Hermione. At 1000 B/24 eight German dive bombers and two high level bombers attacked. Their bombs fell close the escorting ships. HMS Hermione shot down one dive bomber. The three ships arrived at Malta early in the afternoon.

The main body of the convoy meanwhile continued on its way unhindered after the attacks of the motor torpedo boats except for an attempt by three torpedo planes around 0700 hours. They dropped their torpedoes at a safe distance when fired on by the destroyers in the screen ahead. According to the orders Rear-Admiral Syfret was to leave the convoy now, if there was no threat from Italian surface forces, and go on to Malta with the cruisers and some of the destroyers. They were to land the passengers and stores, complete with fuel and return to Force H as soon as possible. The remaining destroyers were to accompany the transports to Malta. They too were to join Force H as soon as possible. Rear-Admiral Syfret felt easy about the surface danger as all Italian ships were reported in harbour the day before, but he was anxious about the threat to the convoy from the air. He decided to go ahead with the cruiser but leave all destroyers with the convoy so at 0745 B/24, HMS Edinburgh, HMS Arethusa and HMS Manxman left the convoy and pressed ahead at high speed to Malta where they arrived at noon the same day. The transports and the destroyers arrived about four hours later. They had been attacked only once by a torpedo plane since the cruisers separated.
Return passage of the warships of force X to make rendez-vous with Force H.

In the evening HMS Edinburgh, HMS Arethusa, HMS Hermione and HMS Manxman sailed together followed by five destroyers; HMS Cossack, HMS Maori, HMS Sikh, HMAS Nestor, HMS Foxhound, later the same evening. The destroyers overtook the cruisers in the morning of the 25th. The sixth destroyer, HMS Farndale, had to be left at Malta due to defects (condenser problems). All ships made rendez-vous with Force H to the north-west of Galita Island at 0800 B/25.
Movements of Force H after it parted from the convoy.

After parting with the convoy in the evening of the 23rd, Vice-Admiral Somerville had taken force H westward at 18 knots until the afternoon of the 24th going as far west as 03°30’E. He then turned back to meet Admiral Syfret, also sending from HMS Ark Royal six Swordfish aircraft which left her in position 37°42’N, 07°17’E at 1000 B/25. After their junction Forces H and X made the best of way towards Gibraltar. Fighter patrols of HMS Ark Royal shot down a shadowing aircraft soon after the fleet had shaped course to the westward, losing a Fulmar in doing so. However another aircraft had meanwhile reported the fleet.

High level bombers appeared from the east and torpedo bombers from the north at 1100 hours. HMS Ark Royal at that moment had four fighters in the air and sent up six more. They prevented the bombing attack shooting down three aircraft out of eight at a cost of two Fulmars, while the ships watched the enemy jettison their bombs 15 miles away. The torpedo attack came to nothing too for the enemy gave up the attempt and retired while still several miles from the fleet. Two days later, on the 27th, the fleet reached Gibraltar.
The movements of the seven empty ships coming from Malta.

Six of the transports / tankers left Malta for Gibraltar in the morning of the 23rd, escorted by HMS Encounter. The seventh ship, tanker Svenor grounded while leaving harbour and was held up for some hours. At dusk, when a few miles from Pantelleria, the six ships devided into pairs according to their speed. HMS Encounter initially escorted the middle pair but joined the leading ships in the evening of the 24th when past the Galita Bank.

Italian aircraft, both high level bombers and torpedo planes, attacked all these ships on the 24th to the southward of Sardinia. They made their first attempt on the second pair of transports and HMS Encounter. Four torpedo planes attacked at 1230 B/24 and four bombers at 1250 B/24. No ships were hit though the bombs fell close. Next came the turn for the leading pair, which were attacked further westwards by two bombers that came singly at 1330 B/24 and 1400 B/24. The second plane nearly hit HMS Breconshire. Finally when the third pair of ships reached about the same position in the evening they were attacked by torpedo planes and the Hoegh Hood was damaged but she managed to arrive at Gibraltar only a few hours after her consort on the 27th. The last ship, the one that had been delayed at Malta, arrived on the 28th. (23)
22 Jul 1941 At 0435 B/22, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) dived 10 miles south of the intended patrol position 37°35'N, 15°35'E.

At 1810 B/22, in position 37°54'N, 15°35'E faint HE of high speed screws was picked up bearing 060°. It was drawing slowly to the right. Nothing could be seen though. It may have been a motor torpedo boat proceeding close inshore. (16)

23 Jul 1941 At 0915 B/23, in position 37°57'N, 15°34'E, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) sighted three ships bearing 096° at a range of 9000 yards. Enemy course was 310° at 6 knots. They had not been seen earlier due to the glare of the sun. Course was altered to attack. The convoy consisted of two merchant vessels of about 2000 tons in line ahead with another ship, believed to be an armed merchant cruiser of about 4000 tons. The AMC was inshore of the merchant vessels !

This convoy was made up of the German transport Tinos (2827 GRT, built 1914) and Italian transport Bolsena (2384 GRT, built 1918) screened by the Italian armed merchant cruiser Egitto (3329 GRT, built 1928). They sailed from Crotone at 1915 B/22, speed 9 knots, Tinos for Reggio (Calabria) and Bolsena for Catania. At 1007 B/23, they were off Punta Pellaro and at 1043 B/23, Egitto ordered Tinos to enter Reggio while Bolsena followed her to Catania where they arrived at 1615 B/23.
0935 B/23, The enemy altered course to 340°.

0948 B/23, The convoy was now bearing 078°. Range 5500 yards. Broke off the attack to commence attack on a large vessel seen bearing 016° at a range of 8000 yards. Enemy course was 185°. This vessel was escorted by a torpedo boat.

This was the Vichy-French hospital ship Canada (9684 GRT, built 1912) escorted through the Straits of Messina by an (unidentified) Italian vessel (they were sighted by the armed merchant cruiser Egitto at 1000 B/23). This escort was probably an auxiliary vessel. Unfortunately, the Messina command files makes no mention of the passage of Canada.

1000 B/23- This new target was now bearing 034° at a range of 3 miles. Enemy course was now 160°. The target was identified as the hospital ship Tevere (Italian, 8448 GRT, built 1913) (which was thus incorrect) and the attack was broken off. (16)
24 Jul 1941 At 0320 B/24, in position 37°56'N, 15°34'E, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) picked up HE bearing 080°.
At 0323 B/24, a ship was sighted bearing 095° at a range of about 9000 yards. The enemy was steering east.
At 0328 B/24, the ship was lost from sight.

Possibly this was a ship belonging to the Vertunno convoy, made up of the Italian transports Vertunno (3159 GRT, built 1905), Beppe (4859 GRT, built 1912) and the Italian salvage vessel Artiglio (386 GRT, built 1908) escorted by the auxiliary Lago Zuai (783 GRT, built 1940). They had sailed from Messina at 1930 B/23 and arrived at Crotone at 1705 B/24.
At 1129 B/24, in position 37°56'N, 15°33'E, sighted what is thought to be a Brioni-class armed merchant cruiser bearing 100° at a range of 6 miles. Enemy course was 330° at 15 knots.
This was possibly the Italian armed merchant cruiser Zara (1976 GRT, built 1931) which sailed at 1000 B/23 from Brindisi for Naples.

At 1246 B/24, in position 37°54'N, 15°34'E, two distant explosions were heard. A large column of water was sighted bearing 090° at a range of about 7 miles. [This was HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) making an attack in the billet to the east of HMS Unique.]
From 1248 to 1515 B/24, distant depth charging was heard.
At 1455 B/24, in position 37°56'N, 15°37'E, three destroyers were sighted bearing 130° at a range of 4 miles. Enemy course was 340° at 15 knots.

This were the torpedo boats Aretusa and Rosolino Pilo. There was no third destroyer with them [Both the Aretusa and Rosolino Pilo reports were checked]. The floating dock G.O. 22 towed by the tugs Tesco and Valente and escorted by the torpedo boat Aretusa had sailed from Taranto at 1430 B/22. They were joined by two MAS boats at 2023 B/22 until 2110 B/22 when they left. The torpedo boat Rosolino Pilo sailed from Messina at 2030 B/23 to reinforce the escort and joined them at 0610 B/24. At 1235 B/24, an escorting aircraft signalled torpedoes fired on the convoy, an explosion was seen on the coast and another close to the floating dock. They reached Messina at 1940 B/24 (Pilo) and 2125 B/24 (the remainder of the convoy).
At 1510 B/24, a Cant. Z.501 floatplane was seen accompanying the destroyers. HMS Unique went deep and ran out to the south-west. (16)

25 Jul 1941 At 0500 B/25, in position 37°58'N, 15°31'E, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) picked up HE bearing 120° at 200 revs. HMS Unique was deep at that moment. The HE moved right and faded out on 030°. It is believed to have been a destroyer proceeding north along the Sicilian coast.
Possibly this was the Italian destroyer Lanciere. She had sailed from Taranto at 1518 B/24 and arrived at Messina at 0628 B/25.
At 0710 B/25, in position 37°58'N, 15°34'E, a destroyer was seen bearing 205° at a range of 5 miles. The enemy was steering 020° at high speed.
This was the Italian torpedo boat Rosolino Pilo. She sailed from Messina for an A/S sweep at 2130 B/24 and returned at 0825 B/25.
At 0750 B/25, in position 37.59'N, 15.35'E, a two-masted fishing boat, flying a black ball, was seen bearing 060° at a range of 3 miles. Enemy course was 140°.
At 0840 B/25, in position 37.57'N, 15°35'E, an armed motor yacht of about 100 tons was seen bearing 055° at a range of 3 miles. Enemy course was 340° at 12 knots.
At 0853 B/25, a small armed yacht was seen bearing 070° at a range of 3 miles. Enemy course was 280°.
At 0935 B/25, in position 37°55'N, 15.37'E, a three-masted schooner was seen bearing 090° at a range of 3 miles. Enemy course was 310°.

At 1410 B/25, in position 37°58'N, 15°35'E, a small two-masted schooner was seen bearing 120° at a range of 5 miles. The enemy was laying stopped head to sea. This vessel was lost to sight at 1745 B/25 and had disappeared when HMS Unique returned to the same position later in the evening.
[Besides the destroyer and torpedo boat identified, we have not been able to identify the remaining vessels sighted on this day.] (16)
26 Jul 1941 At 0610 B/26, in position 37°57'N, 15°32'E, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) sighted a motor torpedo boat bearing 115° at a range of 3 miles. Enemy course was 260° at 18 knots.
Probably this was MAS 424 which sailed from Messina for patrol at 2320 B/25 and returned at 0750 B/26.

At 0749 B/26, in position 37°54'N, 15°33'E, a small two-masted schooner (which had also be seen the previous day at at 1410 B/25) was seen bearing 240° ar a range of 3 miles. Enemy course was 070°. The schooner was proceeding towards the Italian coast, but returned around 1000 B/26 and remained in the vicinity of position 37°55'N, 15°34'E. Later in the afternoon she moved to the vicinity of position 37°54'N, 15°36'E. At dusk she moved off towards the Sicilian coast on course 250°. No fishing gear was observed out and it may well have been an auxiliary A/S vessel.
At 1450 B/26, in position 37°58'N, 15°32'E, a Turbine-class destroyer was seen bearing 260° at a range of 4 miles. Enemy course was 030°.
This was the destroyer Euro which sailed from Porto Empedocle at 0445 B/26 and reached Messina at 1645 B/26.
At 1744 B/26, in position 37°58'N, 15°34'E, an object was sighted which was believed to be a submarine, bearing 250° at a range of 5 miles. Unique altered course to 320° and closed at speed.
At 1805 B/26, the vessel was identified as the Italian submarine chaser Albatros steering 020° at 15 to 18 knots.

This could not have been the Albatros though, as she was at Messina at the time and did not sail before 4 August 1941. This was probably the torpedo boat Cigno which sailed from Catania at 0835 B/26 for an A/S patrol and reached Messina at 1900 B/26.
At 2100 B/26, HMS Unique was ordered to patrol in area 'Q', a rectangle, with it's south-west corner in position 36°40'N, 15°10'E and sides running 350° for 90 miles and 080° for 35 miles. (16)
27 Jul 1941 In the morning, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) investigated the Sicilian coast between Taormina and Cape Ali (further north) with a view to carrying out train wrecking operations. (16)
28 Jul 1941 At 1230 B/28, in position 37°54'N, 15°24'E, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) sighted a small armed yacht bearing 290° at a range of 3 miles. Enemy course was 035°, speed around 6 knots.

At 2130 B/28, the Senior Officer Submarines signal timed 1655 B/28 was received which stated that HMS Unique may also operate for shore operations in the area originally assigned to HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) as this submarine had now left patrol. (16)

29 Jul 1941 At 0100 B/29, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) landed a Folbot-party near Cape Ali from a position offshore (38°01'N, 15°27'E) with the objective to place explosive charges on the train tracks. At 0138 hours the Folbot-party (Corporal R.H. Brown and Lance Corporal J.R.A. Ferguson) returned and Unique retired to seaward. At 0325 B/29 an explosion was seen on the land as a train must have hit the charges.
According to Italian sources the locomotive and 13 wagons were derailed (some of them carrying German stores) and the engineer was wounded but the line was re-established at noon.

At 0110 B/29, during the operation, a 2000 tons merchant vessel was seen bearing 060° at a range of 4 miles. Enemy course was 210° at 10 knots. No attack was made as the target was small and probably in ballast. Also the chances of re-embarking the train wrecking party would have been seriously jeopardised. After the vessel had passed it was seen that she was escorted by a destroyer to seaward.

This was possibly the German transport Tinos (2827 GRT, built 1914) escorted by the auxiliary Lago Tana (78 GRT, built 1940). They had sailed from Messina at 2330 B/28 and arrived at Catania at 0530 B/29.
The destroyers Ascari, Carabiniere, Lanciere and Corazziere were exercising near Messina at the time and the destroyer seen could perhaps have been one of them.

During the remainder of the day the Calabrian coast to the east of Cape dell'Armi was investigated to carry out another train wrecking operation. At 2335 B/29, HMS Unique began to close the coast. (24)

30 Jul 1941 At 0003 B/30, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) landed a Folbot-party near position 37°55'N, 15°54'E (near Bova Marina) with the objective to place explosive charges on the train tracks. At 0123 hours the Folbot-party returned and Unique retired to seaward. No explosions were seen however before Unique cleared the area. At 0330 B/30, the starboard main engine developed a serious defect.
[Nothing was found Italian archives reporting an incident in this area.]

At 1130 B/30, in position 37°35'N, 15°45'E a four-masted schooner was sighted bearing 230° at a range of 5 miles. She was laying stopped. She was drifting to leewards and HMS Unique only closed very slowly during the day.
At 1400 B/30, the Senior Officer Submarines Malta's signal timed 1123 B/30 was received which ordered HMS Unique to resume patrol off the Straits of Messina.

At 1730 B/30, it was decided to attack the large schooner. She was of about 500 tons and 130 feet long. She appeared to have a Breda machine gun mounted forward, two amidships and possibly one aft as well. Although laying stopped she did not appear to be fishing. A torpedo fired ran under.

1801 B/30 - Fired one torpedo (Mark II) at an armed 4-masted auxiliary patrol schooner in position 37°30'N, 15°35'E from 700 yards. The torpedo ran under. The target hoisted two flags, made a circle and then close Unique's firing position. Another schooner (a smaller two-masted one) joined her and they appeared to be listening. Both departed the area to the south-west at 1850 hours.
At 2000 B/30, HMS Unique set course to proceed northwards to comply with the Senior Officer Submarines Malta's signal timed 1123 B/30 (see above).

At 2139 B/30, the Senior Officer Submarines Malta's signal timed 0335 B/30 was received. This started that an enemy submarine would leave Augusta around 2015 B/30 on a course of 105°. HMS Unique was at that moment about 20 miles north of the enemy submarine's expected track and with only one main engine operational it was impossible to reach an attack position.
This information was due to ULTRA and referred to the Italian submarine Dessiè which left August at 2000 B/30 for Leros. (16)

31 Jul 1941 At 0545 B/31, in position 37°43'N, 15°33'E, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) sighted a large passenger ship bearing 074° at a range of 8 nautical miles. This ship was identified as the Vichy-French hospital ship Canada (9684 GRT, built 1912) proceeding on course 330° at a speed of 10-12 knots.
At 1120 B/31, in position 37°56'N, 15°37'E, a small motor yacht was seen laying stopped. No armament was visible.

At 1328 B/31, in position 37°53'N, 15°33'E, a motor torpedo boat (thought to be of the MAS 438-class) was seen bearing 300° at a range of 5 miles. The enemy was moving slowly north, turning later and proceeding slowly southwards. (16)
1 Aug 1941 At 0610 B/1, in position 37°57'N, 15°33'E, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) sighted a motor torpedo boat bearing 105° at a range of 5 miles. Enemy course was 340° at 8 knots.

At 1005 B/1, in position 37°57'N, 15°35'E, a motor torpedo boat was seen bearing 090° at a range of 2 miles. She was laying stopped. She proceeded at 1058 B/1 on course 220° at 20 knots. HMS Unique altered course away to evade but two minutes later the enemy stopped again.
At 1115 B/1, the enemy was bearing 120° at 1000 yards. HMS Unique went to 70 feet and ran out to the south-west.

At 1300 B/1, HMS Unique returned to periscope depth. The motor torpedo boat was seen bearing 020° at a range of 2 miles. A fishing boat, flying a red flag was seen bearing 350° at a range of 2 miles. Both were laying stopped. HMS Unique retired to the south.

At 1306 B/1, the Senior Officer Submarines signal timed 1044 B/1, was received ordering HMS Unique to stay on patrol until 2100 B/2 to intercept a cruiser and a tanker bound from Messina to Crete. [We have no clue as to which ships this signal could have referred to.]

At 2115 B/1, HMS Unique surfaced and sighted three large ships, all well lit, bearing 320° at a range of 5 miles. Their course was 160° at 12 knots. These were identified as three Vichy-French ships on which orders had been received not to molest them (Senior Officer Submarines Malta's signal timed 1057 B/31). They were led by a darkened motor torpedo boat which turned back toward Messina half an hour later.

This convoy from France to Syria and back to France was made up of the transports Alsina (8404 GRT, built 1922), Djenne (8790 GRT, built 1931) and Koutoubia (8790 GRT, built 1931). This was 'Z convoy', which had departed They had sailed from Toulon on 30 July and arrived at Beirut on 5 August. They then left Beirut on 7 August and arrived at Marseille on 13 August. During the return passage the Alsina carried 1495 passengers, on board the Djenne were 1583 passengers and on board the Koutoubia were 1650 passengers. In total there were 4728 passengers on board the ships of the convoy during the return passage from Syria to France of which 4338 were military personnel. (16)

2 Aug 1941 At 0217 B/2, in position 37°48'N, 15°48'E, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) heard the noise of a fast revving engine, fairly loud which passed down the starboard side from west to east. Visibility was just over 2 miles. Nothing was sighted an no HE was heard.

At 0335 B/2, in position 37°49'N, 15°52'E, ships were sighted bearing 025° at a range of about 4 miles. Enemy course was 270° at about 10 knots. The vessels were difficult to distinguish in the poor visibility and against the land but they appeared to be a 3000 tons merchant vessel and two smaller merchant vessels escorted by two destroyers or torpedo boats. HMS Unique closed at 6 knots but it was decided not to attack in order to not give away the submarines position as the expected cruisers and tanker mentioned in the Senior Officer Submarines signal timed 1044 B/1 had not yet been sighted. HMS Unique dived to avoid being sighted.

Perhaps the ships sighted were the Italian tanker Cesco (6161 GRT, built 1917), the Italian auxiliary Lago Tana (783 GRT, built 1940) and the Italian destroyer Riboty which sailed from Taranto at 0450 B/1, were reinforced by the motor torpedo boat MAS 424 and arrived at Messina at 1719 B/2. The description of the convoy does not quite match though but as said in the patrol report the ships were difficult to see.
At 1303 B/2, in position 37°51'N, 15°53'E, sighted two destroyers bearing 075° at a range of 4 miles. Enemy course was 270°. HMS Unique altered course to keep clear and went deep.
[We have been unable to identify these 'destroyers'. None should be operating in the area as far as we could find out.]

At 1400 B/2, on returned to periscope depth one destroyer was sighted bearing 310° at a range of 4 miles. Enemy course was 090°. A Cant. Z 501 flying boat was flying very low from the direction of the destroyer straight towards HMS Unique which quickly went deep again.
[We have been unable to identify this 'destroyer'. None should be operating in the area as far as we could find out.]
At 1450 B/2, HMS Unique returned to periscope depth. There was nothing in sight.
At 2100 B/2, HMS Unique left patrol and set course for Malta. (16)
4 Aug 1941 Around 0700 B/4, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) ended her 12th war patrol (10th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (16)
8 Aug 1941 HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) is docked in No.2 graving dock at the Malta Dockyard. (25)
13 Aug 1941 HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) is undocked. (25)

16 Aug 1941 Around 1830 B/16, HMS Unique (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) departed Malta for her 13th war patrol (11th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off Tripoli, Libya in 'Area T' (south of 33°20'N, and between 11°40'E and 13°10'E).
Lt. Hezlet had taken over from Lt. Collett for this patrol in order to give Lt. Collett a rest.
On departure for patrol a practice attack was made on HMS Abingdon (Lt. G.A. Simmers, RNR). (26)
For the daily and attack positions of HMS Unique during her 13th war patrol see the map below.

17 Aug 1941 In a signal timed 2015 B/17, the Senior Officer Submarines Malta informed HMS Unique (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN), HMS P 32 (Lt. D.A.B. Abdy, RN) and HMS P 33 (Lt. R.D. Whiteway-Wilkinson, DSC, RN) of the following; Second convoy (referred to in the signal timed 1601 B/14) due Tripoli about noon/18. When passed HMS P 33 return to Malta as in my signal timed 1144 B/15. HMS Unique and HMS P 32 proceed to patrol in the vicinity of positions 33°20'N, 13°03'E and 33°17'N, 13°14'E respectively to intercept three large motor ships and six destroyers. Enemy speed speed 17.5 knots. Enemy due Tripoli about noon/20.

The fast convoy referred to was the convoy made up of the troop transports (liners) Marco Polo (12272 GRT, built 1912), Neptunia (19328 GRT, built 1932), Oceania (19507 GRT, built 1933) and Esperia (11398 GRT, built 1920). Their escort was made up of the Italian destroyers Ugolino Vivaldi, Nicoloso Da Recco, Alfredo Oriani and Vincenzo Gioberti. (the Italian torpedo boat Orione could not join them due to engine defects). This convoy sailed from Naples at 2250 B/18-0140 B/19.

This convoy was reinforced by the torpedo boat Giuseppe Dezza (sailed from Trapani at 0910 B/19 until Tripoli) off Cape San Vito at 1330 B/19 and at 1450 B/19 from Marettimo by the destroyers Maestrale, Grecale and Scirocco (the destroyers sailed from Palermo at 0730 B/19, the first two escorted the convoy until 0100 B/20 and returned to Palermo, while the third went on to Tripoli).
This convoy reached Tripoli at 1230-1415 B/20. (27)
18 Aug 1941 Around 0500 B/18, HMS Unique (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) arrived in position 33°20'N, 13°10'E (to the north of Tripoli) to intercept a 8.5 knot convoy expected at Tripoli around 1630 B/18.

This referred to a convoy from Naples to Tripoli which was made up of the transports Caffaro (Italian, 6476 GRT, built 1924), Giulia (Italian, 5921 GRT, built 1926), Maddalena Odero (Italian, 5545 GRT, built 1921), Marin Sanudo (Italian, 5958 GRT, built 1926), Nicolò Odero (Italian, 6003 GRT, built 1925) and the tanker Minatitland (Italian, 7651 GRT, built 1941). It was escorted by the Italian destroyers Freccia, Euro, Dardo and the Italian torpedo boats Procione and Pegaso. The torpedo boat Giuseppe Sirtori sailed from Palermo at 1600 B/16 to reinforce the escort. Off Trapani, Procione was detached and entered this port at 1300 B/28. Eight miles south of Lampedusa, Maddalena Odero was torpedoed twice by RAF bombers at 2045 B /17. Pegaso towed her to Lampedusa while Sirtori looked for survivors. Giuseppe Sirtori, Pegaso and the stricken vessel entered this port at 1215 B/18 (or 1020 B/18, sources give diffent times). At 1300 B/18, Maddalena Odero was hit by a bomb from a RAF aircraft and blew up. Pegaso turned back, arriving at Trapani at 0625 B/19, while Giuseppe Sirtori sailed from Lampedusa at 2015 B/18 for Porto Empedocle. The remainder of the convoy reached Tripoli at 1700 B/18-1800 B/18.

The convoy had been attacked in position 39°35'N, 13°18'E by HrMs O 23 (Lt.Cdr. G.B.M. van Erkel, RNN) at 1004 A/16 (1115 B/16 according to the Italians) from long range but all torpedoes missed. The torpedoes were sighted by Freccia and by a CANT Z.501 seaplane.
Between 0650 B/18 and 0700 B/18, HMS Unique called HMS P 33 (Lt. R.D. Whiteway-Wilkinson, DSC, RN) on S.S/T. There was no reply.
At 0810 B/18, HMS P 33 was called again on S.S/T but yet again no reply was forthcoming.
At 0820 B/18, when in position 33°19'30"N, 13°10'30"E, S.S/T communication was established with HMS P 32 (Lt. D.A. Abdy, RN) which bore 180° at 180 miles.
Between 0930 B/18 and 1330 B/18 distant explosions were heard approaching. The convoy was coming !

At 1459 B/18, the Senior Officer Submarines Malta's signal timed 1401 B/18 was received which ordered HMS Unique to proceed with despatch to position 33°00'N, 13°03'E. At 1216 B/18 the convoy had been in position 306° - Tripoli - 56 miles, course 130° at 8 knots.
At 1517 B/18, As the convoy could not be intercepted submerged HMS Unique surfaced and set course 194° at full speed.
At 1534 B/18, three aircraft were seen patrolling ahead as though escorting a convoy. As they were about 10 miles away, continued to close on the surface.
At 1600 B/18, a schooner was sighted laying stopped 8 miles on the starboard bow.
At 1617 B/18, HMS Unique dived for a large aircraft flying very low and towards. Continued to close the ordered position at 5 knots submerged.
At 2115 B/18, HMS Unique surfaced and withdrew to the north-west along approach route of convoy. (16)

19 Aug 1941 Around 0800 B/19, HMS Unique (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) commenced patrolling in the vicinity of position 33°20'N, 13°03'E in accordance with the Senior Officers Submarines Malta's signal timed 1101 B/18 to intercept a fast, 17.5 knot, convoy which was due at Tripoli at noon on 20 August. [For the composition of this convoy see the event for 20 August 1941.]
At 0920 B/19, HMS P 32 (Lt. D.A.B. Abdy, RN) was called on S.S/T. There was no reply.
At 1547 B/19, distant explosions at 15 minute intervals were heard to the westwards. Altered course to 270°.

At 1655 B/19, masts and funnels of four merchant vessels were seen bearing 275° at a range of about 10 miles. Enemy course was 345°, speed about 10 knots. A single mast, thought to have been from a destroyer, was seen ahead of the convoy. A flying boat was circling the convoy.

This convoy was made up of the transports Andrea Gritti (Italian, 6338 GRT, built 1939), Francesco Barbaro (Italian, 6343 GRT, built 1940), Rialto (Italian, 6099 GRT, built 1927), Sebastiano Venier (Italian, 6311 GRT, built 1940) and Vettor Pisani (Italian, 6339 GRT, built 1939). They were escorted by the Italian destroyers Freccia, Euro, Dardo and the Italian torpedo boat Procione. The convoy sailed from Tripoli at 1245 B/9-1345 B/19 and arrived at Naples at 0800 B/21-0945 B/21 (except Procione who arrived at 1610 B/21).
At 1700 B/19, course was altered to the north to make and enemy report after dark.
At 2120 B/19, HMS Unique surfaced and proceeded northwards at 15 knots.

At 2216 B/19, an enemy report was passed. Course was altered to the southward to patrol in position 33°05'N, 13°03'E to intercept the southbound 17.5 knot convoy in accordance with the Senior Officers Submarines Malta's signal timed 1316 B/19. (16)

20 Aug 1941 HMS Unique (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian liner / troop transport Esperia (11398 GRT, built 1920) 11 nautical miles bearing 318° of the Tripoli lighthouse, Libya in position 33°03'N, 13°03'E.

Esperia had been in convoy that was further made up of the Italian liners / troop tranports Marco Polo (12272 GRT, built 1912), Neptunia (19328 GRT, built 1932) and Oceania (19507 GRT, built 1933). They were escorted by the Italian destroyers Ugolino Vivaldi, Nicoloso Da Recco, Vincenzo Gioberti and Alfredo Oriani. The convoy had sailed from Naples at 2255 B/18- 0140 B/19 for Tripoli. The escort was reinforced by the destroyers Maestrale, Grecale (both until 0100 B/20) and Scirocco who sailed from Palermo at 0730 B/19 (except Grecale who followed at 0325 B/20) and the torpedo boat Giuseppe Dezza who sailed from Trapani at 0925 B/19. The torpedo boat Partenope had sailed from Tripoli at 0620 B/20 to reinforce the escort. The convoy, minus Esperia arrived at Tripoli at 1245 B/20-1415 B/20.
According to Italian sources four were killed and twelve were missing, 1139 survivors (including 53 wounded) were picked up.

0636 B/20 to 0725 B/20 - Sighted the torpedo boat Partenope, two MAS boats and three minesweepers. They were passing about a mile inshore of Unique. From their movements the position of the swept channel was fixed and Unique was placed near the entrance to await the convoy of troopships that was reported.
0956 B/20 - In position 33°03'N, 13°03'E sighted four liners bearing 305°, range about 8 nautical miles. Enemy course was 155°. Started attack.
1010 B/20 - Range was now 6500 yards. Of the escort a Partenope-class torpedo boat was sighted an also a Navigatori-class destroyer but most likely there were many others.

1019 B/20 - After getting inside the escort screen fired a salvo of four torpedoes from 650 yards against one of the liners. Upon firing Unique cleared the torpedo tracks and set course to the north at 90 feet. Shortly afterwards three torpedoes were heard to hit the target.
1037 B/20 to 1137 B/20 - 15 Depth charges were dropped but none were close.
1230 B/20 - Came to periscope depth, only a fling boat and a schooner were seen. Went deep again and reloaded the torpedo tubes.
1620 B/20 - Called HMS P 32 (Lt. D.A.B. Abdy, RN) on S.S/T. There was no reply.
1815 B/20 - While at periscope depth in position 33°13'N, 12°56'E sighted a CANT Z-501 flying boat alter towards. Went deep and altered course.
1816 B/20 - A depth charge exploded on the port quarter.
1818 B/20 - Another depth charge exploded very close on the port beam. Some minor structural damage was caused but she was able to remain on patrol. Went to 150 feet.
According to Italian sources the aircraft was a CANT Z.501 of 145^ Squadriglia piloted by Midshipman De Solem who dropped two bombs and claimed to have hit the submarine. (16)
21 Aug 1941 HMS Unique (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) was patrolling off Zuara parallel to the coastal shipping route.

At 1317 B/21, the Senior Officer Submarines Malta's signal timed 1231 B/21 was received ordering HMS Unique to intercept the 17 knot convoy leaving Tripoli at 1700 B/21. It was not possible to reach position 33°07'N, 13°07'E as ordered in time but position 33°14'N, 12°47'E covering the route along which the convoy arrived yesterday and the route from position 32°53'N, 12°53'E to Kerkennah could be reached by proceeding on the surface at full speed for two hours. So Unique surfaced at 1340 B/21 and ran to the north-east at full speed.

The signal referred to the Italian liners / troop transports Marco Polo (12272 GRT, built 1912), Neptunia (19328 GRT, built 1932), Oceania (19507 GRT, built 1933) and Esperia (11398 GRT, built 1920). Their escort was made up of the Italian destroyers Ugolino Vivaldi, Nicoloso Da Recco, Alfredo Oriani and Vincenzo Gioberti. (the Italian torpedo boat Orione could not join them due to engine defects). The convoy sailed from Naples at 2250 B/18-0140 B/19.

The convoy was reinforced by the torpedo boat Giuseppe Dezza (sailed from Trapani at 0910 B/19 until Tripoli) off Cape San Vito at 1330 B/19 and at 1450 B/19 from Marettimo by the destroyers Maestrale, Grecale and Scirocco (the destroyers sailed from Palermo at 0730 B/19, the first two escorted the convoy until 0100 B/20 and returned to Palermo, while the third went on to Tripoli).
The convoy reached Tripoli at 1230-1415 B/20.
At 1440 B/21, HMS Unique had to dive for an aircraft but was apparently not sighted.
At 1510 B/21, HMS Unique had just surfaced when it was noticed that the submarine was leaving an oil slick.
At 1600 B/21, HMS Unique dived and proceeded further to the north-east while submerged.
At 1857 B/21, A Generali-class torpedo boat was seen 5 miles to the eastward proceeding towards Tripoli.

At 2015 B/21, HMS Unique was at periscope depth when an explosion was heard. A thin column of water was sighted about one mile away through the periscope. This was repeated four times. It appeared that Unique was bombed from the air although no aircraft were sighted. Two minutes later, HE was heard and fire was opened on the periscope with a gun. Unique was taken to 90 feet and course was altered. For the next 45 minutes HMS Unique was hunted but no depth charges were dropped.
At 2250 B/21, HMS Unique surfaced and withdrew to the northward to investigate the oil leak. (16)
22 Aug 1941 At 0700 B/22, HMS Unique (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) left patrol to return to Malta. The oil leak was still present giving away the presence of the submarine.
At 2256 B/22, a W/T report was passed informing Malta of their return. (16)
23 Aug 1941 Around 1900 B/23, HMS Unique (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN) ended her 13th war patrol (11th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (16)
25 Aug 1941 HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) is docked in No.2 graving dock at the Malta Dockyard to repair some structural damage sustained on 20 August. (25)
1 Sep 1941 HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) is undocked. (28)
5 Sep 1941 Around 1500 B/5, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) departed Malta for her 14th war patrol (12th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the Tyrrhenian Sea.
On departure two practice attacks were made on HMS Gloxinia (Lt.Cdr. A.J.C. Pomeroy, RNVR). (29)
For the daily positions of HMS Unique during her 14th war patrol see the map below.
6 Sep 1941 At 2136 B/6, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) received Capt. S. 10's signal timed 1210 B/6 reporting a northbound convoy due at Marittimo at 0430 B/7.

This referred to the convoy made up of the Italian transports Col di Lana (5891 GRT, built 1926), Ernesto (7399 GRT, built 1914) and the Italian tanker Poza Rica (7799 GRT, built 1940). They were on passage from Tripoli to Naples. The convoy was escorted by the Italian destroyers Nicoloso Da Recco, Freccia, Folgore and Strale.

This convoy had sailed from Tripoli at 1244-1600 B/5. At 2355 B/6, Ernesto was torpedoed by an enemy bomber in position 37°08'N, 11°53'E (circa 20 miles north of Pantelleria), she was escorted by Strale and the torpedo boat Circe (which had sailed at 0255 B/7 from Trapani to assist her and joined at 0700 B/7). Strale had taken the damaged ship in tow but this proved difficult and signalled she required the assistance of tugs so the tugs Montecristo, Costante and Marsigli were sailed from Trapani accordingly. They all arrived at Trapani at 1725-1745 B/7. The remaining two merchant ships and three destroyers arrived in Naples at 0132-0420 B/8.
At 2330 B/6, HMS Unique altered course to the north-east along the Cape Bon to Naples convoy route. (30)
7 Sep 1941 At 0540 B/7, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) dived and patrolled in the vicinity of position 'L' (north of Marittimo, 38°16'N, 12°08'E) during the day.
At 2035 B/7, HMS Unique surfaced and set course for Cape St. Vito. (16)
8 Sep 1941 At 0540 B/8, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) dived 8 miles north of Cape St. Vito which was then closed after HMS Unique patrolled eastwards along the coastal route.

At 1035 B/8, just north of Cape St. Vito, in position 38°14'N, 12°43'E, a trawler and two fishing boats were seen bearing 210 at a range of 4 miles. Enemy course was approximately 360°. These vessels proceeded slowly up the coastal route. It was thought this may be auxiliary A/S vessels. [We have not been able to identify these vessels.]
At 1200 B/8, HMS Unique went to 80 feet and ran out to the northward to evade the above vessels as they were now approaching.

At 1255 B/8, now in position 38°16'N, 12°47'E, HMS Unique returned to periscope depth. The vessels had apparently passed since smoke could be seen along the coastal route to the eastward. A three-masted schooner with an auxiliary engine was seen bearing 195° at a range of 2 miles. Enemy course was 245°.

At 1837 B/8, in position 38°16'N, 12°58'E, a destroyer and two small merchant vessels were seen bearing 262° at a range of 6 miles. Enemy course was 085°. The destroyer, thought to be a Maestrale-class, was making very wide zigzags across the bows of the two merchant vessels which were in line ahead on a steady course and making good about 6 knots.
At 1926 B/8, both merchant vessels were seen to be of 1500 tons or less and in ballast. It was therefore decided that they were not worth a torpedo and the approach was broken off.

Possibly these ships were larger, as these may well have been the damaged transport Ernesto (7399 GRT, built 1914) towed by the tugs Marsigli, Montecristo and Terracina and the transport Florida II (3313 GRT, built 1905) towed by the tug Costante. These were screened by the destroyer Strale and torpedo boat Circe. They had sailed from Trapani at 1415 B/8 [the torpedo boats sailed at 1143 B/8 and 1148 B/8 respectively, probably on A/S search ahead) and arrived in Palermo at 1215-1227 B/9). Perhaps the smaller tugs were not seen from the range of 6 miles.
At 2025 B/8, HMS Unique surfaced and set course to the eastward. (16)

9 Sep 1941 At 0058 B/9, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) received the Capt. S. 10's signal timed 0030 B/9 which reported that a 8.5 knot southbound convoy five merchant ships and six destroyers from Naples was expected at Marittimo around 0400 B/10.

This must have referred to the convoy made up of the transports Bainsizza (Italian, 7933 GRT, built 1930), Caffaro (Italian, 6476 GRT, built 1924), Giulia (Italian, 5921 GRT, built 1926), Nicolò Odero (Italian, 6003 GRT, built 1925), Nirvo (Italian, 5270 GRT, built 1919) and Tembien (Italian, 5584 GRT, built 1914) and their escort made up of the Italian destroyers Alfredo Oriani, Fulmine and the torpedo boats Procione, Pegaso and Orsa. According to ULTRA information, the convoy was expected to sail at 0330 B/9 (see file DEFE3/689, frame 81), however Italian sailing orders dated 8 September indicate that the convoy was expected to leave at 0000 B/10. It actually sailed at 2330 B/9-0210 B/10 and arrived in Tripoli at 2347 B/12-1128 B/13.
At 0115 B/9, course was altered to the westward to intercept.
At 0545 B/9, HMS Unique dived.

At 1137 B/9, north of Cape St. Vito, in position 38°16'N, 12°47'E, a small merchant vessel and a fishing boat were seen bearing 210° at a range of 4 miles. Enemy course was 265°. Course was altered to close.

The Pugliola (2074 GRT built 1917) and Corso Fougier (1348 GRT built 1906) had sailed 0600 B/9 in company from Palermo for Cagliari, escorted by auxiliary Francesco Morosini (2423 GRT, built 1928). Perhaps small merchant vessel sighted was one of these.
At 1200 B/9, the attack was broken off as the merchant vessel was considered too small to attack from long range.
At 2040 B/9, Unique surfaced and continued her passage to the Marittimo area.
Note: the naval water tanker Istria (2900 GRT, built 1924) sailed from Palermo unescorted at 2230 B/8 and reached Trapani at 0625 B/9. It appears she slipped by HMS Unique undetected. (16)
10 Sep 1941 HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) commenced patrolling near position 'L' (38°16'N, 12°08'E). She kept down moon of the expected line of advance of the convoy.
At 0540 B/10, HMS Unique dived.
At 2030 B/10, HMS Unique surfaced. Nothing had been seen all day. (16)

11 Sep 1941 At 0008 B/11, north of Cape St. Vito, in position 38°16'N, 12°30'E, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) sighted smoke bearing 115° moving right. Course was altered to 160° to intercept on the coastal route. The target mover rapidly right and as HMS Unique was then 10 miles from the coastal route the chase was given up as the situation was hopeless.
At 0158 B/11, in position 38°17'N, 12°52'E, a dark object was sighted bearing 135° at a range of 7000 yards. This was thought to be a submarine and course was altered and an attack was started.
At 0202 B/11, the target was identified as being a trawler moving very slowly westwards along the coast. The attack was broken off.
At 0540 B/11, HMS Unique dived and patrolled on the coastal route near Palermo.

At 0815 B/11, north of Palermo, in position 38°15'N, 13°25'E, three fishing vessels were seen bearing 150° at a range of 3 miles. They were presumed to be auxiliary minesweepers. They were in line abreast and were all flying two back balls and a large flag.
At 0830 B/11, the minesweepers altered course to 290°.
At 1120 B/11, the minesweepers rounded Cape Gallo and disappeared to the westward.

At 1430 B/11, in position 38°12'N, 13°35'E, three fishing vessels were seen bearing 180° at a range of 4 miles. They were flying the usual signals and were apparently mine sweeping on course 170°. They later turned to 360°.
At 2025 B/11, HMS Unique surfaced and set course to the north-eastward.
[We have not been able to identify any of the vessels sighted on this day.] (16)

12 Sep 1941 At 0540 B/12, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) dived and patrolled eastwards along the presumed convoy route north of Stromboli running 075° from position 'L' (north of Marittimo, 38°16'N, 12°08'E).
At 2020 B/12, HMS Unique surfaced. Nothing had been sighted while patrolling submerged.
At 2315 B/12, HMS Unique turned north-west along the direct route from Cape Vaticano to Naples an which route she patrolled on the 13th. Nothing was seen though. (16)
14 Sep 1941 At 0540 B/14, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) dived 10 miles to the south of Capri Island.

At 0625 B/14, on coming to periscope depth 20 minutes before sunrise in position 40°24'N, 14°15'E, an Oceania-class liner escorted by two destroyers was sighted bearing 090° at a range of 7 miles. Enemy course was 160°. From her position and course it appeared that she had come through the Bocca Piccola. Unique now set course to patrol to the south of the Bocca Piccola.
This was Vulcania (24648 GRT, built 1928) escorted by the destroyers Nicoloso Da Recco and Antonio Da Noli. They had sailed from Naples at 00400-500B/14 and arrived in Taranto at 0830-0930 B/15.

At 0710 B/14, a barquentine proceeding under the power of an auxiliary engine was seen bearing 295° at a range of 3 miles. Enemy course was 090°. Unique altered course to 180° to avoid. The barquentine, thought to be an auxiliary A/S vessel, later moved off the the south-west.

At 1110 B/14, in position 40°27'N, 14°20'E, three fishing boats flying black balls and large flags were seen bearing 350° at ranges between 7 - 8 miles. They appeared to be sweeping westwards just to the north of the Bocca Piccola.
At 1330 B/14, in position 40°28'N, 14°23'E, a 3000 tons merchant vessel was seen bearing 335° at a range of 10 miles. Enemy course was 050° at 12 knots.
Possibly the armed merchant cruiser Adriatico (1976 GRT, built 1931 GRT) which sailed from Naples at 0630 B/14 for Messina. [the course is a bit of a problem but she may have followed the coast].
At 1355 B/14, in position 40°28'N, 14°22'E, a small coaster was sighted bearing 008° at a range of 6.5 nautical miles. Enemy course was 105°.
Possibly this was the auxiliary Paola Z. Podestà (863 GRT, built 1882) which sailed from Naples at 0500 B/14 for Messina.

At 1940 B/14, in position 40°27'N, 14°24'E, two big merchant vessels and one small ship were seen bearing 260° at a range of about 7 miles. They were extremely difficult to observe in the dusk but were moving northward and course was probably 330°. This would have taken them to the west of Capri and not through the Bocca Piccola.
[We have not been able to identify these vessels.]
At 2025 B/14, HMS Unique surfaced and set course to patrol on the original suspected convoy route, 147° from close west of Capri Island. (16)

15 Sep 1941 At 0135 B/15, south-west of Capri Island, in position 40°27'N, 14°09'E, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) picked up HE of 150 revs bearing 035°. One minute later a destroyer was seen bearing 040° at a range of 4 miles. Enemy course was 160° at 15 knots.
This was the Sebenico which sailed at 2350 B/14 from Naples for an A/S sweep (ahead of the arrival of the convoy seen at 0621 B/15) and returned at 1850 B/15.
At 0535 B/15, HMS Unique dived 4 miles to the east of Capri Island.
At 0618 B/15, in position 40°32'N, 14°08'E, HE was picked up on bearing 190°.

At 0621 B/15, a convoy was sighted bearing 190° at a range of 7 miles. Enemy course was 045° at 14 knots. The convoy consisted of two merchant vessels of about 10000 tons and one of about 6000 tons. They were escorted by five destroyers. A Cant Z.501 flying boat was patrolling overhead. Commenced attack.

This must have been the convoy made up of the transports Rialto (Italian, 6099 GRT, built 1927), Sebastiano Venier (Italian, 6311 GRT, built 1940) and Vettor Pisani (Italian, 6339 GRT, built 1939) escorted by the italian destroyers Alfredo Oriani, Fulmine and the torpedo boats Procione, Pegaso and Orsa which had come from Tripoli which they had departed around 1745-1924 B/13 and arrived at Naples at 0845-0948 B/15.

It was however not possible to get into firing range before the convoy passed through the Bocca Piccola and the attack was broken off at 0700 B/15. HMS Unique now set course to patrol just to the eastward of the approach route to the Bocca Piccola.

At 0852 B/15, in position 40°29'N, 14°13'E, two merchant vessels were seen bearing 080° at a range of 7 miles. Enemy course was 305° at 7 knots. The merchant vessels were of 1500 and 2500 tons. These ships were also heading for the Bocca Piccola. They could not be closed sufficiently to attack.

These were probably the transport Zena (5219 GRT, built 1914) escorted by the auxiliary Filippo Grimani (3431 GRT, built 1928) which had sailed from Messina at 1100 B/14 for Naples and was expected to arrive at 1100 B/15.

At 1040 B/15, in position 40°29'N, 14°15'E, three minesweepers were seen bearing 045° at a range of 5 miles. Enemy course was 150°. They swept up and down four times between the Bocca Piccola and position 40°29'N, 14°23'E before returning to harbour.
At 1110 B/15, a 3-masted sailing vessel was seen bearing 225° at a range of 8 miles. It was laying stopped remaining in the vicinity of this position all day. Obviously an auxiliary A/S vessel.

At 1257 B/15, in position in position 40°27'N, 14°18'E, HMS Unique had just come to periscope depth, when an Italian submarine of the Adua-class was sighted bearing 310°, steering 185°, apparently exercising with a 'Generale'-class destroyer / torpedo-boat. This was almost certainly the Italian submarine Corallo exercising at the time with the torpedo-boat Generale Antonio Cascino.

HMS Unique was unable to gain an attacking position and was thwarted by the presence of the destroyer / torpedo-boat. Later a trawler was also seen with the submarine and destroyer / torpedo boat. An attack had not been possible to the erratic movements of the ships during the exercises.
At 1746 B/15, in position 40°29'N, 14°15'N, a motor torpedo boat was seen bearing 008° at a range of 2 miles. She was laying stopped, probably listening. Course was altered away to the southward.
At 2025 B/15, HMS Unique surfaced and set course to position 'L''(north of Marittimo, 38°16'N, 12°08'E). She proceeded along the expected convoy route but nothing was seen on the 16th.
[We have not been able to identify the other vessels sighted on this day.] (31)

17 Sep 1941 At 0605 B/17, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) dived 12 miles to the north of Marittimo Island and then patrolled close westward of that island. She surfaced at 2025 B/17. Nothing had been seen all day. (16)
18 Sep 1941 At 0001 B/18, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) left patrol to return to Malta.

At 0512 B/18, south-west of Marsala, in position 37°41'N, 12°12'E, HMS Unique sighted two merchant vessels and two destroyers bearing 060° at a range of 4 miles. Both ships were about 2000 to 3000 tons and were not more than half laden. They were zigzagging 30° either side of a mean course of 210°. The destroyers were zigzagging about 50° on either side of this course at 15 knots.

At 0520 B/18, HMS Unique was 2000 yards ahead of the other wing destroyer and dived as it was too light from the approaching dawn to continue the attack on the surface. The destroyer could not be observed through the periscope due to a bad horizon and the HE was reported all round, HMS Unique was forced to go deep. The convoy passed close down the port side (500 to 1000 yards) and the destroyer passed very close astern probably only at 100 yards. On return to periscope depth the rear merchant vessel was seen at a range of 3000 yards on a 160° track and the attack had to be abandoned.

The ships sighted by HMS Unique must have been part of a convoy which was made up of the transports Caterina (4786 GRT, built 1920), Col di Lana (5891 GRT, built 1926), Marin Sanudo (5081 GRT, built 1926) and the tanker Minatitland (7651 GRT, built 1940) escorted by the destroyers Freccia, Folgore, Dardo and Euro. This convoy had sailed from Naples at 0125-0445 B/17 [a fifth transport, the Castelverde (6666 GRT, built 1921) did not sail due to mechanical defects) for Tripoli. The Col di Lana was torpedoed by a RAF bomber at 0435 B/18 in position 190° - Punta Marsala – 3 miles. She was towed by the tugs Liguria and Montecristo and screened by Dardo, and brought to Trapani where she was beached at 1330 B/18. Dardo subsequently rejoined the convoy which reached Tripoli at 1736-1758 B/19. (16)

19 Sep 1941 Around 0930 B/19, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) ended her 14th war patrol (12th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. It had been a most frustrating patrol with quite a lot of sightings but no opportunities for attack. (16)
5 Oct 1941 HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) is docked in No.2 graving dock at the Malta Dockyard. (32)
7 Oct 1941 HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) is undocked. (32)

9 Oct 1941 Around 1300 A/9, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) departed Malta for her 15th war patrol (13th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to a position south-east of Cape Bon, Tunisia (36°35'N, 11°28'E) to intercept a southbound convoy. As the convoy passed well to the east, Unique was later ordered to her patrol area in the Tyrrhenian Sea.

The convoy referred to must have been the one made up of the transports Bainsizza (Italian, 7933 GRT, built 1930), Casaregis (Italian, 6485 GRT, built 1924), Giulia (Italian, 5921 GRT, built 1926), Nirvo (Italian, 5270 GRT, built 1919), Zena (Italian, 5219 GRT, built 1914) and the tanker Proserpina (Italian, 4870 GRT, built 1926). It was escorted by the destroyers Granatiere, Fuciliere, Bersagliere and Alpino and was on passage from Naples to Tripoli.

The Nirvo (5164 GRT, built 1919) and the torpedo boat Generale Antonino Cascino had sailed from Trapani at 1645 B/9 to join the convoy. However Nirvo had engine defects which forced to return to Trapani still escorted by Cascino and arrived there at 0230 B/10. Cascino then rejoined the convoy. Bainsizza also developed engine defects and had to turn back to Trapani.
At 1350 B/10, about 30 miles south of Pantelleria, a Savoia 79 bomber escorting the convoy sighted a submarine which was most probably HMS Unique but was unable to attack it.
At 2330 B/10, in position 34°52'N, 12°22'E (42 miles south of Lampedusa), Zena was hit by an aircraft torpedo and abandoned, 1 killed, Alpino picked up 120 survivors.
At 0610 B/11, Casaregis was hit by a Swordfish torpedo bomber of 830 Squadron (FAA). Three hours later, she was finished off by gunfire (118 rounds from Bersagliere).
Giulia and Proserpina screened by the five escorts arrived at Tripoli at 1530-1723 B/11.
10 Oct 1941 At 0030 A/10, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) altered course to close the convoy's mean line of advance at right angles, with the intention of diving on the line before dawn.
[For the composition and other details of this convoy see 9 October 1941.]

At 0441 A/10, south of Pantelleria, in position 36°06'N, 11°55'E, a destroyer was sighted bearing 135° at a range of 3 miles. Enemy course was 350° at 12 knots. HMS Unique dived since the moon was bright. Faint HE was heard and the destroyer was heard to pass to the eastward.
This was probably the torpedo boat Pallade which sailed from Zuara at 1600 B/9 and arrived at 0330 B/11 in Naples.

At 1120 A/10, south-west of Pantelleria, in position 36°14'N, 11°40'E, a faint wisp of smoke was seen bearing 070°. This was probably the expected convoy which as later reports showed had passed about 12 miles to the eastward.

At 1632 A/10, Capt. S.10's signal timed 1524 A/10 was received which ordered HMS Unique to proceed in execution with her originally intended patrol orders, to patrol in the Tyrrhenian Sea. Course was set to pass to the eastward of Pantelleria and then dive to pass through the Italian minefield off south-west Sicily during daylight on the 11th. (16)

11 Oct 1941 At 0900 A/11, in position 37°28'N, 12°22'E, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) picked up faint HE (90 revs) bearing 280°. The HE was moving south. Ten minutes later HMS Unique was at periscope depth but nothing could be seen.
At 0935 A/11, more faint HE was heard on bearing 290°. It was also moving south. (16)
12 Oct 1941 At 0503 A/12, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) dived and then patrolled the coastal route north-east of Cape St. Vito.

At 0817 A/12, north of Cape St. Vito, in position 38°15'N, 12°46'E, a Navigatori-class destroyer was sighted bearing 300° at a range of only one mile. Enemy course was 230° at 18 knots. Unique went to 80 feet and took avoiding action. Ten minutes later HE had faded but at 0830 A/12 two depth charges were heard exploding, a long way off though.
This was the Nicoloso Da Recco which sailed from Naples at 2300 B/11 and arrived at Trapani at 1043 B/12.
At 1855 A/12, HMS Unique surfaced and set course for Naples. (16)
14 Oct 1941 HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) made a torpedo attack on an Italian armed merchant cruiser south of Naples Bay. A torpedo hit is claimed as the ship stopped and appeared to be damaged.

This was the armed merchant cruiser Adriatico (1976 GRT, built 1931) escorting the water tanker Cassala (1771 GRT, built 1902) from Naples to Messina. All four torpedoes missed. One was avoided by the Adriatico and two by the Cassala. The torpedo boat Giuseppe Missori and the 3rd A/S Group were ordered to hunt the submarine but found nothing.
0900 A/14 - Entered area 'N' (north of 40°00'N, 14°15'E).
1210 A/14 - While to the south of the Bocca Piccola, in position 40°26'N, 14°20'E sighted smoke bearing 350° in the Gulf of Naples.

1236 A/14 - Sighted two merchant vessels bearing 335°. Range was about 8 nautical miles. They were altering course to pass south through Bocca Piccolo. Unique altered course to attack. The leading ship was identified as a Citta di Genova-class or Birmania-class. The ship was painted white and was most likely an armed merchant cruiser. The second ship was a 'funnel-aft' merchant vessel of about 5000 tons in ballast.
1314 A/14 - Fired four torpedoes from 3500 yards.

1330 A/14 - Observed the AMC stopped with a black streak down her starboard side. It appears she was hit. The merchant vessel meanwhile had turned round and was making back to Naples. Unique went deep again and retired to the south-east.

1337 A/14 - Heard an explosion, which was not considered to be a depth charge. It is claimed a hit had been obtained and the target was damaged. Unique was now at 130 feet and rand out to the south and then the east.
1445 A/14 - Very distant depth charging was heard. They were dropped at 10 minute intervals until 1540 A/14.
1640 A/14 - Returned to periscope depth. There was nothing in sight.
1645 A/14 - Went to 80 feet and commenced reloading the torpedo tubes.
1702 A/14 - Three distant depth charges were dropped.
1853 A/14 - On coming to 40 feet before surfacing in position 40°19'N, 14°12'E fairly loud HE (170 revs) was picked up bearing 050°. It was drawing right to 085° but then drawed left.
1922 A/14 - HE faded out on bearing 025°.
1926 A/14 - Surfaced.
1936 A/14 - Sighted a dark object bearing 135° at a range of about one mile. It was moving northwards and is thought to have been a motor torpedo boat.
1937 A/14 - Dived to avoid being sighted. No HE was picked up.
2112 A/14 - Surfaced.
2115 A/14 - Heard faint HE (160 revs) bearing 100°. It was drawing north. Nothing could be sighted though.
2359 A/14 - Set course for Licosa Point to patrol on the coastal route between Messina and Naples the following day.
Lt. Collett was later criticised by Capt. S.10 for this action as he should have reloaded one torpedo tube very quickly to finish off the damaged ship. (16)

15 Oct 1941 At 0203 A/15, in position 40°11'N, 14°12'E, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) sighted a destroyer bearing 140° at a range of 4 miles. Enemy course was 320°. HMS Unique altered course away. Probably this was the torpedo boat Enrico Cosenz which had sailed from Naples at 1938 B/14 on an A/S hunt and returned at 1005 B/15.
At 0205 A/15, the destroyer altered course to 270°.
At 0208 A/15, HMS Unique dived to avoid being sighted.
At 0212 A/15, HE (160 revs) faded out on bearing 250°.
At 0400 A/15, HMS Unique surfaced.
At 0424 A/15, in position 40°12'N, 14°20'E, a destroyer was sighted bearing 350° at a range of 5 miles. Enemy course was 190°.
At 0426 A/15, HMS Unique again dived to avoid being sighted.
At 0520 A/15, in position 40°13'N, 14°21'E, HE (170 revs) was picked up bearing 230°. It was moving slowly left.

At 0555 A/15, HE was still audible on bearing 120°. On coming to periscope depth a Navigatori-class destroyer was sighted bearing 104° at a range of 3 miles. The enemy was steering to the north-west. This was probably still the torpedo boat Enrico Cosenz though. [This was a ship with three funnels but the forward funnel was very close to the forward superstructure so Lt. Collect probably saw only two funnels which was the same number as the Navigatori-class destroyers had.]
At 0600 A/15, HMS Unique was at 60 feet. The destroyer moved slowly left down the port side.
At 0640 A/15, HE faded out on bearing 350° which was the direction of the Bocca Piccolo.

At 0650 A/15, in position 40°13'N, 14°23'E, two masts of a small sailing vessel were seen bearing 294°. Range was 7 miles. The enemy was moving slowly backwards and forwards. This was most likely one of the auxiliary A/S schooners operating off Naples Bay.
At 1400 A/15, course was altered to the south-east parallel to the coastal route. (16)
16 Oct 1941 At 0505 A/16, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) dived and commenced to patrol parallel to and to seaward of the coastal route between Licosa Point and Cape Palinuro.

At 0740 A/16, in position 40°02'N, 15°08'E, a small minelayer / minesweeper (Crotone-class or RD-class) and a fishing boat were seen bearing 030° at a range of 3 miles. Enemy course was 310° at 9 knots.
[We have not been able to indentify this 'minelayer / minesweeper '.]
At 1845 A/16, HMS Unique surfaced.
At 1946 A/16, in position 40°10'N, 14°45'E, a destroyer was sighted bearing 310° at a range of 3 miles. Enemy course was 110°.
At 1948 A/16, HMS Unique dived to avoid being sighted. Seven minutes later the destroyer passed close ahead of HMS Unique doing 230 revs. HE later faded out on bearing 140°.

This was probably the torpedo boat Giuseppe Missori which sailed from Naples at 1615 B/16 and arrived at Messina at 0955 B/17. She was escorting the tanker Thorsheimer (Italian, (former Norwegian), 9955 GRT, built 1935).
At 2120 A/16, HMS Unique surfaced. (16)
17 Oct 1941 At 0005 A/17, after having completed charging, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) closed the Capri - Licosa coastal route and proceeded to the north-west along it.
At 0450 A/17, in position 40°30'N, 14°27'E a destroyer was sighted bearing 250° at a range of 6 miles. Enemy course was 030°.
This was possibly the torpedo boat Lira which had sailed from La Spezia at 0730 B/16 and arrived at Messina at 1310 B/17.
At 0500 A/17, HMS Unique dived.
At 0555 A/17, in position 40°30'N, 14°24'E, a two-masted sailing vessel was seen. This vessel was moving slowly backwards and forwards and thus could very well be an auxiliary A/S vessels.
From 0837 A/17 to 0900 A/17, a regular ticking noise was heard on bearing 250°. Nothing was in sight on this bearing.
From 0920 A/17 to 0955 A/17, the ticking noise was heard again on bearing 270°. Again nothing could be sighted.

At 1656 A/17, south-south-east of the Bocca Piccola, in position 40°20'N, 14°27'E, a large merchant vessel was seen bearing 257° at a range of 21000 yards. Enemy course was 160°. Only her funnel and masts were seen so no escort would have been visible from this range.
This was probably the hospital ship Arno (Italian, 8024 GRT, built 1924) which sailed at 1530 B/17 for Augusta.

At 1855 A/17, HMS Unique surfaced and then set course to return to Malta leaving patrol. She was to proceed through position 38°40'N, 11°50'E (north-north-west of Marittimo) in accordance with Capt. S.10's signal timed 1255 A/15. (16)

17 Oct 1941 In a signal timed 1145 A/17, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) and HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) were informed that HMS Rorqual (Lt. L.W. Napier, RN) and HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) would proceed from position 37°16'N, 12°56'E to position 38°00'N, 11°44'E during daylight/19. HMS Rorqual would then proceed towards Cavoli Island and HMS P 34 was to operate east of 12°00'E and south of 38°40'N. [This patrol area for HMS P 34 is however a bid strange as HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) departed Malta on the 19th to operate in exactly this patrol area. It therefore seems likely that HMS P 34 had been ordered to patrol somewhere else, most probably further to the east, most probably between Cape St. Vito and Palermo, but no signal on this is listed in the war diary summaries.] (33)
19 Oct 1941 At 0555 A/19, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) dived and patrolled to the westward of position 'J' (38°00'N, 11°44'E, west of Marittimo Island).
At 1700 A/19, it was no longer possible to patrol at periscope depth due to the bad weather conditions so HMS Unique went deep.
At 1725 A/19, in position 37°58'N, 11°27'E, tried to contact HMS Rorqual (Lt. L.W. Napier, RN) which should pass through the area around this time. There was no reply.
1805 A/19, HMS Unique surfaced to continue the passage to Malta. (16)
20 Oct 1941 At 0515 A/19, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) dived and commenced to pass south-eastwards through the Italian declared mine zone (QBB 65).
From 1030 A/19 to 1400 A/19, HMS Unique attempted to communicate with HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) in accordance with Capt. S.10's signal timed 1531 A/18.
From 1415 A/19 to 1440 A/19, while in position 198° - Cape Granotola Light - 4.5 miles, communicated with HMS Upright on S.S/T bearing 162° - 18500 yards.
At 1815 A/19, HMS Unique surfaced and continued her passage to Malta. (16)
21 Oct 1941 Around 0700 A/21, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) ended her 15th war patrol (13th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (16)

1 Nov 1941 Around 1830 B/1, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) departed Malta for her 16th war patrol (14th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off Benghazi, Libya in area 'C' (south of 32°45'N, and east of 18°00'E).

2 Nov 1941 At 2153 A/2, south-south-east of Malta, in position 34°46'N, 14°48'E, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) sighted a yellow flare bearing 320° at a range of 2 miles. This was considered to be a sighting signal from a U-boat. HMS Unique dived but no HE was picked up.
[No submarine or MAS boat was known to be operating in this area. Probably it was an aircraft flare.] (19)
5 Nov 1941 At 1800 B/5, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) entered area 'C' in position 32°45'N, 18°15'E. (19)
6 Nov 1941 From 0400 B/6 to 0550 B/6, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) patrolled on the surface north of Benghazi near position 32°36'N, 19°57'E.
At 0550 B/6, HMS Unique dived and proceeded eastwards.
From 0910 B/6 to 1200 B/6, HMS Unique patrolled in the vicinity of position 32°38'N, 20°04'E.
At 1200 B/6, HMS Unique proceeded to the south-east to reconnoitre the northern end of the suspected mined area, observed earlier by HMS Thrasher (Lt. H.S. Mackenzie, RN). No activity was observed.
At 1640 B/6, HMS Unique withdrew 5 miles to the north-westward.

At 1820 B/6, HMS Unique surfaced and patrolled in the vicinity of position 32°41'N, 20°03'E until 0600 B/7 with the view of discovering the new route by shipping proceeding to Benghazi from the northward. (19)
7 Nov 1941 At 0505 B/7, in position 32°38'N, 20°02'E, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) picked up HE bearing 095°. The contact was moving northward. Nothing could be seen though.
At 0700 B/7, HMS Unique was in position 32°32'N, 19°58'E, to intercept a small convoy expected to arrive at Benghazi.

This must have referred to the convoy made up of the Italian transport Bosforo (3648 GRT, built 1929) and the German transport Savona (2120 GRT, built 1934) which were to proceed from Brindisi to Benghazi escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Pegaso. The convoy sailed from Brindisi at 2300-2400 B/6. At 1030-1210 B/8, it was attacked by enemy bombers in position 290° - Argostoli – 58 miles, Savona was hit by a bomb and an enemy aircraft crashed on her, killing two German soldiers, the bodies of two aviators found in the wreck. She could only steer manually. Bosforo was also hit by a bomb and was slightly damaged, two Italian soldiers were killed. The convoy was diverted to Navarino where it arrived at 1045-1100 B/9. Bosforo escorted by Pegaso sailed from there at 1800-1830 B/10 and arrived at Benghazi at 0730-0900 B/12. Savona escorted by the torpedo boat Francesco Stocco sailed from Navarino at 2330 B/11 for Patras where they arrived at 1500 B/12.?

At 0900 B/7, having sighted nothing of the convoy, HMS Unique proceeded eastwards for further reconnaissance of the northern end of the suspected mined area, observed earlier by HMS Thrasher (Lt. H.S. Mackenzie, RN).
From 1400 B/7, HMS Unique patrolled near the vicinity of position 32°38'N, 19°48'E. (19)
8 Nov 1941 At 0800 B/8, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) received Capt. S.10's signal timed 0133 B/8 that a merchant vessel was expected to arrive at Bengazi between 2300 B/8 and dawn on the 9th.
[Apparently, an error as no enemy ship appears to have entered Benghazi around the time given.]
At 1230 B/8, HMS Unique proceeded to the southward to patrol to the west of 19°51'E. In anticipation that southbound convoys to Bengazi may still be using the meridian of 19°51'E.

At 1700 B/8, in position 32°31'N, 19°45'E, the Italian hospital ship Arno (7785 GRT, built 1912) was seen proceeding on course 320° at 14 knots. Arno had sailed from Benghazi at 1330 B/8 for Naples but was diverted to pick up survivors of the Duisburg convoy.

At 2005 B/8, received the Capt. S.10's signals timed 1927 B/8 (probable new convoy route to Benghazi) and 1849 B/8 (convoy of 5 merchant vessels and 4 destroyers prossible arrival time at Benghazi area between 0100 B/9 and dawn on the 10th).

This referred to the Duisburg convoy which was later decimated by 'Force K'. It consisted of the transports Duisburg (German 7389 GRT, built 1928) and the Maria (Italian, 6339 GRT, built 1926), San Marco (Italian, 3076 GRT, built 1919), Sagitta (Italian, 5153 GRT, built 1919) and the tanker Minatitlán (Italian, 7651 GRT, built 1940) screened by the destroyers Maestrale, Fulmine and Euro. It had transited the Straits of Messina around 0240 B/8. The convoy was later joined by the transports Rina Corrado (Italian, 5180 GRT, built 1918) and Conte di Misurata (Italian, 5014 GRT, built 1908) escorted by the destroyers Grecale, Libeccio and Alfredo Oriani which sailed from Messina at 1400 B/8. (19)
9 Nov 1941 From 2100 B/8 to 0150 B/9, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) patrolled in the vicinity of position 32°31'N, 19°45'E.
From 0150 B/8 to 1400 B/9, HMS Unique patrolled in the vicinity of position 32°34'N, 19°48'E.
Nothing was sighted all day. (19)
10 Nov 1941 From 1620 B/9 to 1230 B/10, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) patrolled on line 140° from position 32°42'N, 19°43'E.
At 1230 B/10, HMS Unique proceeded to the south-east to reconnoitre probable swept channel 157° from position 32°32'N, 19°55'E. Nothing was seen though.
At 1600 B/10, in position 32°27'N, 20°01'E, HMS Unique turned to the north-west to resume patrol near position 32°42'N, 19°43'E.
At 1858 B/10, Capt. S.10's signal timed 1735 B/10 was received that a small convoy was expected to arrive at Benghazi from the west shortly after 0700 B/11.
At 2200 B/10, HMS Unique proceeded to patrol north-west of Benghazi in the vicinity of position 32°25'N, 19°35'E to intercept this small convoy.

The signal must have referred to the convoy made up of the transports Cadamosto (Italian, 1010 GRT, built 1905) and the Spezia (German, 1825 GRT, built 1924) that was escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Centauro. The convoy sailed from Tripoli at 0540 B/9 and arrived at Benghazi 1512 B/11. (19)

11 Nov 1941 At 0600 B/11, HMS Unique proceeded to the eastward to reconnoitre the north-west end of the swept channel, 311°, 15 miles from Benghazi light. Nothing was heard or seen from the expected convoy coming from Tripoli.
At 1100 B/11, HMS Unique turned to the north-west to resume patrol to cover the northern convoy route to and from Benghazi.

At 1359 B/11, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1221 B/11 was received which stated that one merchant vessel was expected to arrive from the northward at dawn on the 12th. Proceeded to position 32°38'N, 19°51'E to intercept this expected merchant vessel.

The merchant vessel in question was the Bosforo (Italian, 3648 GRT, built 1929). She was escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Pegaso. They had departed from Navarino Bay at 1800-1830 B/10 and arrived at Benghazi at 0730-0900 B/12. (19)

12 Nov 1941 At 0058 B/12, HMS Unique arrived in position 32°38'N, 19°51'E. A 4000 tons merchant vessels escorted by one destroyer was sighted bearing 000° at a range of 16000 yards. Enemy course was 180° at 9 knots.
This were the transport Bosforo (Italian, 3648 GRT, built 1929) and her escort, the Italian torpedo boat Pegaso. [See 7 and 11 November 1941 for more info on their movements.]
The sea was calm with bright moonlight and when the target had closed to 10000 yards it was decided to continue the attack submerged as HMS Unique would otherwise certainly be sighted.

HMS Unique was approximately 2000 yards off track and the merchant vessel was observed to be steering a steady course. However at 0135 B/11, the enemy altered course to 140° and put HMS Unique in a position 6000 yards on a 140° track. The target was closed at speed as this was thought at first to be a zig and not a navigational alteration of course, and it was impossible to carry out an attack, Unique therefore surfaced and followed the merchant vessel, in the hope of getting another opportunity of attacking.
At 0200 B/12, the merchant vessel disappeared over the horizon having maintained a course of 140° since 0135 B/11.

At 0640 B/12, HMS Unique withdrew to the north-east after diving and remained deep in order to replace no.2 crank pin bearing of the starboard main engine which had run on 11 November, and its removal was necessary in order to effect repairs. A strong sea was now running and periscope depth was difficult to maintain except beam on to the sea.

At 1126 A/12, in position 32°38'N, 19°58'E, HMS Unique, which had just gone deep for an aircraft, heard HE bearing 330°. An aircraft (Caproni Ca.310) was sighted bearing 325°. The enemy was at 300 feet proceeding on course 150°. Came up to periscope depth and sighted three Condottieri 'A'-class cruisers in position 32°43'N, 19°53'E, bearing 330° at a range of 10000 yards. Enemy course was 170° at 25 knots. These were first thought to be three destroyers but were identified at 1140 B/11, as Condottieri 'A'-class cruisers.
This were the Italian destroyers Antonio da Noli, Emanuele Pessagno and Ugolino Vivaldi on a troop transport run to Benghazi.

HMS Unique was approximately 6000 yards off track and an attempt was made to close. On the next return to periscope depth an air escort was observed very close and turning towards. It was thought HMS Unique had been sighted as a heavy sea had forced the boat up to 20 feet.
At 1141 B/12, HMS Unique went to 80 feet.
At 1146 B/12, HMS Unique returned to periscope depth. The approach had to be broken off as the targets were too far off.
At 1855 B/12, HMS Unique surfaced.

At 1949 B/12, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1915 B/12, was received which reported three enemy cruisers steering 170° at 20 knots from Taranto. It was intended to pass an enemy report shortly after surfacing at 1855 hours but it was considered that the three enemy cruisers mentioned in this signal might follow up the others to carry out minelaying operations off Benghazi. The sending of an enemy report was therefore delayed until the following night in order to avoid being fixed by D/F bearings and thus jeopardising further chances of attack.

At 2300 B/12, in position 32°32'N, 19°58'E, a destroyer was sighted bearing 210° at 10000 yards. Enemy course was 060° at 20 knots. Unique endeavoured to close to attack on the surface as there was no moonlight. However the attack was broken off when the enemy turned to the southward at 2325 B/11.
This was probably the Italian torpedo boat Centauro which sailed from Benghazi for an A/S search at 1357 B/12, went to Derna then turned back and arrived at Zuetina at 0956 B/14.

At 2340 B/12, HMS Unique turned to the northward to resume patrol in the vicinity of position 32°43'N, 19°53'E, in ordered to intercept the three enemy cruisers reported in Capt. S.10's signal timed 1915 B/12. It was also considered that the three cruisers sighted in the forenoon would return by the northward convoy route from Benghazi during daylight on the 14th. (19)

13 Nov 1941 At 1905 B/13, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) surfaced and proceeded towards position 32°40'N, 18°00'E in accordance with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0931 B/11 regarding the return route to Malta from patrol. (19)
17 Nov 1941 Around 1645 B/17, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) ended her 16th war patrol (14th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (19)

1 Dec 1941 Around 1700 B/1, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 17th war patrol (15th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol to the southward of the Straits of Messina (Area 'G').
3 Dec 1941 At 0900 B/3, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) entered patrol area 'G' and then proceeded to concentrate on position 37°48'N, 16°05'E (south of Cape Spartivento, Calabria). (19)

6 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 2121 B/6, the Capt. S.10 sent the following signal to HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN), HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) and HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN); Exceptional vigilance is necessary against U-boats, majority southbound passage of Straits probably at night. (34)

7 Dec 1941 At 0415 B/7, in position 37°50'N, 16°14'E, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) heard HE from fast revving propellers bearing 225° which faded out on bearing 140° eleven minutes later. This is thought to have been a motor torpedo boat but nothing was sighted.

At 1352 B/7, in position 37°47'N, 16°00'E, a Curtatone-class or Palestro-class torpedo boat was sighted bearing 270° at a range of 8000 yards. Enemy course was 100° at 15 knots. This vessel circled round the submarines position at ranges from 8000 to 12000 yards and then returned to the westward.

Possibly this was the Italian torpedo boat Giuseppe Sirtori which had sailed from Messina at 0600 B/7 for an A/S sweep and returned at 2345 B/7 [on 5 December, Sirtori had depth charged HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) in the same area and claimed her sunk]. (19)
