9 Dec 1941 At 1400 B/9, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) proceeded to patrol in the vicinity of position 37°51'N, 15°44'E in area 'E' in accordance with Capt. S.10's signal timed 1239 B/9.
At 2243 B/9, fairly loud HE was reported bearing 020°. The volume of the sound appeared to increase and fade every few seconds and no revolutions could be obtained. There was nothing in sight. (19)

10 Dec 1941 At 0120 B/10, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) was in the same position as at 2243 B/9, when a similar noise was heard on exactly the same bearing as before. This noise persisted until 0425 B/10. It is not thought to be a vessel but may be caused by several mountain torrents running over steep shingle banks into the sea near Bova Marina.

At 2045 B/10, south of Cape dell'Armi in position 37°45'N, 15°42'E, an unidentified warship was sighted, possibly a Curtatone-class torpedo boat, bearing 310° at a range of 8000 yards. Enemy course was 105° at 19 knots.
This was most probably the Italian destroyer Corazziere which sailed from Messina at 1845 B/10 and arrived in Taranto at 2100 B/10. (19)

11 Dec 1941 At 0900 B/11, south of the toe of Italy, in position 37°45'N, 15°52'E, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) sighted a Navigatori-class destroyers and a Spica-class torpedo boat bearing 350° at a range of 7 miles. Enemy course was 110° at a speed of approximately 20 knots (from revs). They were in line ahead, one mile apart.
This were most probably the destroyers Lanzerotto Malocello and Saetta which sailed from Trapani at 1645-1737 B/10 and arrived at Taranto at 2146 B/11-0100 B/12.
At 1350 B/11, in position 37°51'N, 15°43'E, an armed yacht with a clipper bow was sighted. The ship was armed by what was thought to be a 3" gun. The enemy was patrolling south of Cape dell Armi.
At 1415 B/11, the armed yacht disappeared to the eastward close inshore. [We have been unable to identify this vessel.]
At 1900 B/11, HMS Unique remained patrolling in position 37°51'N, 15°45'E as two important enemy transport ships were expected to pass southwards through the Straits of Messina.

The expected transports must have been the Fabio Filzi (Italian, 6836 GRT, built 1940) and Carlo del Greco (Italian, 6837 GRT, built 1941) escorted by the destroyers Nicoloso da Recco and Antoniotto Usodimare. which were to proceed from Messina to Taranto. This convoy was attacked by HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) on the 13th which sank both transports near Taranto.

At 2058 B/11, a motor torpedo boat was sighted bearing 050° at a range of only 1000 yards. She was beam on to Unique and was laying stopped. HMS Unique dived to 70 feet. [We have been unable to identify this vessel.]
At 2104 B/11, HE was heard bearing 020°. This sounded like a motor torpedo boat. HE faded at 2120 B/11.
At 2200 B/11, HMS Unique surfaced in position 37°51'N, 15°42'E.

At 2240 B/11, engines of a motor torpedo boat were heard but it was not possible to tell the direction on which the enemy was approaching. Nothing was sighted. Unique dived to 70 feet and the HE was heard to be approaching from the northward. It however faded quickly as if the motor torpedo boat had turned round and was proceeding back to the northwards. (19)
12 Dec 1941 At 0010 B/12, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) surfaced and withdrew to the southward to leave patrol in accordance with Capt. S.10's signal timed 1045 B/10.

At 1410 B/12, when east of Syracuse Sicily, in position 36°53'N, 15°54'E, HMS Unique turned round to return to her patrol position in accordance with Capt. S.10's signal timed 1155 B/12 which informed HMS Unique that enemy heavy ships were expected to proceed southwards through the Straits of Messina during the night of 13/14 December. (19)

13 Dec 1941 At 1235 B/13, south-south-east of Cape dell Armi, in position 37°49'N, 15°44'E, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) sighted what is thought to be the Italian hospital ship Arno (7785 GRT, built 1912) bearing 125° at a range of 4 miles. The hospital ship was proceeding on course 317° at 8 knots.

This was indeed most probably the Arno which had sailed from Navarino at 1530 B/11 and searched for survivors of the Italian transport Calitea (sunk by HMS Talisman (Lt.Cdr. M. Willmott, RN) on the 11th). At 1015 B/12, she was ordered to suspend the search at noon and proceed to Naples where she arrived at 0905 B/14.
One minute later a motor torpedo boat was sighted bearing 190° at a range of 7 miles. The enemy was patrolling to the south-westward. [We have been unable to identify this vessel.]

At 1740 B/13, in position 37°48'N, 15°46'E, two motor boats, type Stefano Turr, were seen bearing 025° at a range of 1.5 miles. They were apparently laying stopped. [We have been unable to identify these vessels.]

At 2231 B/13, in position 37°45'N, 15.51'E, heard and then sighted a destroyer bearing 107° at a range of 1.5 miles. The enemy was approaching at high speed. HMS Unique dived. The Asdic operator had first heard the enemy approaching, as the night was very dark and overcast it is doubtful if the enemy had been seen in time for HMS Unique to have dived on time to escape.

This was probably the Italian torpedo boat Giuseppe Dezza which had sailed from Messina at 1400 B/13 for an A/S sweep (ahead of the sortie by the battleship Vittorio Veneto) and returned at 1000 B/15. (19)

14 Dec 1941 At 0900 B/14, when in position 37°46'N, 15°42'E, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) had just returned to periscope depth after having been put deep by aircraft, when three explosions were heard at approximately 4.5 second intervals. Following these explosions depth charges explosions were heard. HMS Unique went to 24 feet and a good look all round in high power was obtained but there was nothing in sight.

At 0927 B/14, in position 37°45'N, 15°43'E, a battleship with one destroyer was sighted bearing 250° at a range of 7 miles. The destroyer was about a mile astern of the battleship. Enemy course was 127°, speed 195 knots (170 revs). This put HMS Unique at least 8000 yards off track. The target was immediately closed at full submerged speed at 50 feet.
The battleship seen was the Vittorio Veneto which was now making for Taranto after having been damaged by a torpedo.

At 0935 B/14, the enemy was observed on the same course in low power only and an all round look was postponed as there were three fighter aircraft right overhead at a height of approximately 3000 feet. The enemy was closed again at full speed.

At 0943 B/14, loud HE was heard approaching from astern. It was decided not to come to periscope depth for another minute when a counter attack developed from that was apparently two destroyers or torpedo boats and one motor torpedo boat. All three craft sounded as if they were circling round a close range at high speed. Although no depth charges which they were dropping were very close.

At 1007 B/14, a scraping noise was heard on the hull on the starboard side of the engine room followed by a sharp explosion. This appeared to be an indicating charge on a sweep wire and was followed by a number of sharp explosions from small and ineffective depth charges.
By 1015 B/14, it was evident that hunting craft were drawing off to the eastward and at 1100 B/14 all HE had faded.
At 1140 B/14, HMS Unique returned to periscope depth. There was nothing in sight.
At 1600 B/14, in position 37°32'N, 15°47'E, a two-masted steel schooner of about 200 tons was sighted bearing 100° at a range of 5 miles. The schooner, an auxiliary A/S vessel was patrolling.

At 1743 B/14, in position 37°29'N, 15°45'E, HMS Unique established S.S/T communication with HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO, RN) bearing 270° - 19 miles. It was HMS Urge which had torpedoed and damaged the Vittorio Veneto.
At 1840 B/14, HMS Unique surfaced and left patrol to return to Malta. (19)
15 Dec 1941 At 1525 B/15, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) ended her 17th war patrol (15th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (19)
21 Dec 1941 HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) is docked in No.1 graving dock at the Malta Dockyard. (35)
27 Dec 1941 HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) is undocked. (35)
29 Dec 1941 At 1845 B/29, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 18th war patrol (16th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the Gulf of Taranto (Area 'H').

30 Dec 1941 At 0050 B/30, HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) sighted a submarine bearing 315° at a range of 3 miles. P 34 turned towards and closed. The other submarine was subsequently identified as a U-class submarine and thus must be HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) which had departed Malta at the same time as HMS P 34 and which had been in company until around 2217 B/29.
At 0059 B/29, HMS P 34 dived and recognition signals were exchanged with HMS Unique through S.S./T. (36)
1 Jan 1942 At 2200 B/1, in position 39°29'N, 17°25'E, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) entered patrol area 'H'. (19)

2 Jan 1942 At 1446 B/2, in position 40°02'N, 17°00'E, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) heard HE (150 revs) bearing 300°. A Folgore-class destroyer was sighted bearing 280° at a range of 1.5 miles. Enemy course was 180° at 15 knots. HMS Unique dived to 70 feet to evade.
At 1535 B/2, in position 40°02'N, 16°57'E, what is thought to be an Orsa-class torpedo boat was sighted bearing 020° at a range of 3 miles. Enemy course was 160° at 15 knots.
We have been able to find the following movements in this area; The torpedo boat Lince sailed at 1250 B/2 for an A/S sweep and returned at 0212 B/4. This was probably the destroyer seen at 1446 B/2.
The torpedo boat Sagittario sailed at 1355 B/2 for an A/S sweep and returned at 0140/4.
The torpedo boat Orsa sailed at 1415 B/2 for trials and exercises and returned at 1740 B/2. This was probably the Orsa-class torpedo boat seen at 1535 B/2. (19)

4 Jan 1942 At 2204 B/4, [position to be further investigated due to a missing page in the patrol report, position at 2000 B/4, had been 40°02'N, 17°06'E], HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) sighted a destroyer bearing 205° at a range of 3 miles. Enemy course was 010° at a speed of 30 knots. HMS Unique dived to 70 feet to evade surfacing again at 2250 B/4.
This was probably the Italian torpedo boat Antares which sailed from Taranto at 1345 B/3 for an A/S sweep and returned at 0030 B/5.? (37)

5 Jan 1942 HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) attacked the Italian battleship Littorio with torpedoes in the Gulf of Taranto in position 40°07'N, 17°07'E. All torpedoes fired missed their target despite the claim for a hit. The Italian battlegroup Littorio was part of was further made up of the battleships Giulio Cesare, Andrea Doria, heavy cruisers Gorizia, Trento and the destroyers Alpino, Ascari, Aviere, Camicia Nera, Carabiniere, Geniere, Antonio da Noli and Antonio Pigafetta. This battlegroup was returning to Taranto after covering the Monviso convoy.

At the time of the torpedo attack, the Italian battlegroup consisted of the battleships Littorio and Giulio Cesare screened by the destroyers Alpino, Ascari, Carabiniere, Fuciliere, Antonio Da Noli and Antonio Pigafetta which arrived at Taranto at 1700-1807 B/5.
The battleship Andrea Doria had been detached earlier, screened by the destroyers Ascari and Carabiniere, and arrived at Taranto at 0440 B/4, the two destroyers then rejoined the Littorio group.
The heavy cruisers Gorizia and Trento screened by the destroyers Aviere, Geniere and Camicia Nera arrived at Messina at 1544-1700 B/5.
0655 A/5 - Dived in position 40°02'N, 17°10'E.
1329 A/5 - Fairly loud depth charge explosions were heard to the southward. About 25 being dropped between that time and 1345 A/5.
1453 A/5 - Faint HE was heard bearing 140° and three minutes later a warships mast was sighted bearing 130°.
1458 A/5 - In position 40°07'N, 17°'07'E a second look revealed more than one warship.
1500 A/5 - Unique was now right ahead of what appeared to be a large warship (battleship) escorted by one cruiser and five destroyers. Started attack.
1505 A/5 - When coming to periscope depth Unique lost trim before a look could be obtained.
1510 A/5 - HE from an escorting destroyer was heard all round forcing Unique to 50 feet until she had passed.
1524 A/5 - Returned to periscope depth to find that the battleship had changed course many minutes ago.
1530 A/5 - Despite not being in a very good attack position now Unique fired four torpedoes.
1540 A/5 - After approximately 9000 yards of running range a very heavy explosion occurred. It was thought that one of the torpedoes had hit the target (but this was not the case). (19)

6 Jan 1942 At 0200 B/6, [position to be further investigated due to a missing page in the patrol report], HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) sighted a destroyer bearing 280° at a range of 2.5 miles. Enemy course was 180° at a speed of only 4 knots. HMS Unique dived to 60 feet to evade surfacing again at 0410 B/6.

For the identity of this destroyer there are two possibilities; 1) The torpedo boat Sagittario on A/S search sailed from Taranto at 2350 B/4 returned at 2000 B/6. 2) The torpedo boat Lince on an A/S search sailed from Taranto at 2330 B/4 and arrived in Crotone at 1617 B/6. [Unfortunately, we do not have their reports for these sorties which could have shed light more precisely on their movements.]

At 0440 B/6, [position to be further investigated due to a missing page in the patrol report], HMS Unique sighted a patrol vessel bearing 300° at a range of 1.5 miles. The enemy was laying stopped. HMS Unique dived to 80 feet to evade. [We have been unable to identify this vessel.]
At 0805 B/6, in position 39°35'N, 17°25'E, an armed yacht was sighted bearing 130° at a range of 1.5 miles. The enemy was laying stopped. [We have been unable to identify this vessel.] (37)
7 Jan 1942 At 2000 B/7, while in position 39°17'N, 17°33'E, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) left patrol to return to Malta in accordance with Capt. S.10's signal timed 1131 B/7. (19)

8 Jan 1942 At 0245 B/8, east of Cape Colonna, in position 38°59'N, 17°27'E, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) sighted a patrol vessel bearing 060° at a range of 3 miles. The enemy was laying stopped. HMS Unique dived to 80 feet. No HE was picked up. Unique remained dived until 0415 B/8. [We have been unable to identify this vessel.]
At 1438 B/8, in position 38°29'N, 17°11'E, fast HE was heard bearing 160°. HMS Unique came up to 26 feet to have a look through the periscope but nothing was sighted. (19)
11 Jan 1942 Around 0815 B/11, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) ended her 18th war patrol (16th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (19)

15 Jan 1942 Around 1835 A/15, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 19th war patrol (17th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol south of the Straits of Messina (in the south-western half of area 'J').

17 Jan 1942 At 1545 B/2, in position 37°42'N, 15°35'E, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) a three-masted schooner was sighted bearing 045° at a range of 5 miles. The foresail was set. Enemy course was 150° at 14 knots. No HE was heard and 15 minutes later the schooner was observed to have altered course to 085°. She proceeded along the Calabrian Coast towards Cape Spartivento.
Between 1725 B/17 and 1735 B/17, very faint HE was heard bearing 030°. Nothing was seen though. (38)

18 Jan 1942 At 0942 B/18, in position 37°42'N, 15°35'E, HE (120 revs) was heard by HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN). A ship's masts and funnels were sighted bearing 050°. The ship was later identified as the Italian hospital ship Virgilio (11718 GRT, built 1928) proceeding southwards on course 190° at 12 knots. Virgilio had sailed from Naples at 1630 B/17 and arrived in Augusta at 1305 B/18.
At 1115 B/18, very faint HE was heard bearing 300° but nothing was sighted. The HE appeared to be moving to the southward.
At 1858 B/18, while deep in position 37°38'N, 15°36'E, fast HE was heard bearing 325°. On coming to periscope depth nothing was seen. (38)

20 Jan 1942 At 1045 B/20, in position 37°47'N, 15°50'E, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) sighted smoke bearing 090° and very faint HE (140 revs) was heard on the same bearing. HMS Unique closed at speed to investigate.
It turned out to be the Italian hospital ship Arno (7785 GRT, built 1912) proceeding westwards along the Calabrian coast on course 270° at 12 knots.
Actually it was not the Arno as the ship sighted was the Italian hospital ship Aquilea (9448 GRT, built 1914) which had sailed from Tripoli at 1745 B/18 and arrived at Naples at 1210 B/21.

In the evening orders were received to leave patrol so as to arrive at Malta around 0730 B/20. It was decided to proceed southwards from the patrol position in order to patrol off Augusta at dawn the following day. (38)

21 Jan 1942 HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) attacked an Italian submarine with four torpedoes about 25 nautical miles South-East of August, Sicily, Italy in position 36°55'N, 15°38'E. No hits were obtained.
This was the Italian submarine Ciro Menotti (C.C. Ugo Gelli) returning to Augusta from a transport mission to Tripoli but the attack was unobserved.

0700 B/21 - While Unique was in position 36°55'N, 15°38'E HE was heard bearing 110° moving left. Unique was at 70 feet and immediately went to periscope depth but nothing could be seen. HE changed bearing rapidly and it was thought that a destroyer or torpedo boat was nearby. The HE faded out after a few minutes. Later it was thought this might have been a submarine.
0716 B/21 - HE was reported bearing 160° and it was moving slowly to the right.
0720 B/21 - A small dark object was seen on this bearing and all tubes were brought to the ready.
0724 B/21 - It was thought the contact was an enemy submarine. Started attack but a good set up could not be immediately obtained.
0730 B/21 - Now also the high power (attack) periscope malfunctioned. The low power periscope now had to be used.
0737 B/21 - Four torpedoes were now finally fired from 2000 yards. They all missed and the submarine was lost from view at 0748 hours. (38)
22 Jan 1942 Around 0745 B/22, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) ended her 19th war patrol (17th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (38)

2 Feb 1942 Around 2100 B/2, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 20th war patrol (18th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol south of Messina. She is to patrol in area 'J'.

4 Feb 1942 At 2145 B/4, in position 37°20'N, 15°58'E, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) sighted the lights of a hospital ship bearing 230° at a range of approximately 10 miles. The hospital ship was proceeding to the south-east.
This was the Italian hospital ship Aquilea (9448 GRT, built 1914) which had sailed from Naples at 2000 B/3 and arrived in Tripoli at 0915 B/6. (38)

5 Feb 1942 At 1453 B/5, south of Cape Spartivento, Calabria, in position 37°47'N, 16°05'E, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) picked up HE (220 revs) on bearing 330° and moving right. Seven minutes later a Crotone-class minelayer was sighted in the vicinity of Cape Spartivento. Enemy course was 080° at 14 knots. [We have been unable to identify this vessel.] (38)

6 Feb 1942 At 1107 B/6, south of Cape Spartivento, Calabria, in position 37°51'N, 16°04'E, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) sighted a schooner proceeding eastwards. The enemy was approximately two miles from the shore.

At 1400 B/6, in position 37°50'N, 15°52'E, the mast and upperhalf of a funnel of a small merchant vessel were seen proceeding westwards from Cape Spartivento approximately one mile from the shore. A second ship's masts were seen 5 minutes later following one mile astern of the first ship. A Cant Z.501 flying boat was seen patrolling 3 miles to seaward of the ships at a height of 200 feet. The ships were identified as a Citta-class armed merchant cruiser of 2500 tons and a small coaster. HE was not heard due to land noises from the sea breaking on the beach.

These were possibly the transport Nino Padre (Italian, 4171 GRT, built 1913) escorted by the Italian armed merchant cruiser Arborea (4959 GRT, built 1929) and the Italian torpedo boat Giuseppe Dezza (which joined them from Messina). They sailed from Taranto at 0700 B/5, passed Cape Spartivento at 1325 B/6 and arrived at Messina at 1830 B/6.
At 1526 B/6, faint HE was picked up on bearing 260° but nothing could be seen. (38)

7 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1315 B/7, Capt. S.10 ordered HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) to operate in the south-west half of area J from 2000 B/9 and that HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) was to operate in north-east half of area J. (39)

8 Feb 1942 At 0010 B/8, near Cape Spartivento, Calabria, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) picked up HE (120 revs) that was moving left. Nothing could be seen but it was considered this was a destroyer proceeding from Taranto to Messina. [We have been unable to place a destroyer or torpedo boat in this position around this time.] (38)

9 Feb 1942 At 2130 B/9, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) received Capt. S.10's signal timed 1011 B/9, ordering her to establish patrol in the Gulf of Taranto in position 40°00'N, 17°17'E by 0800 B/12, to prevent a southerly movement of the Italian Fleet between 12 and 15 February. After having completed charging the batteries course was set to comply with these orders at 2345 B/9. While proceeding to her new patrol position HMS Unique was to remain within 30 miles from the Italian coast. (40)
12 Feb 1942 At 1010 B/12, in position 40°00'N, 17°18'E, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) sighted smoke bearing 342°.

At 1115 B/12, the Italian tanker Lucania (8106 GRT, built 1902) was identified bearing 340° at a range of 4 miles. Enemy course was 155° at 10 knots. Orders had been received to allow this vessel to pass unmolested. [She was sunk by HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) later that day].
At 1835 B/12, in position 40°01'N, 17°19'E, HE (140 revs) was heard bearing 260°. This was probably a destroyer or torpedo boat but the HE was faint and nothing could be seen.

At 2002 B/12, when in position 39°59'N, 17°20'E, HMS Unique was at 30 feet about to surface when fast HE (400 revs) was heard approaching on bearing 030°. This was a motor torpedo boat which passed overhead 15 seconds later. HE faded out at 2013 B/13 on bearing 160°.
At 2025 B/12, in position 39°58'N, 17°21'E, HMS Unique surfaced. (38)

13 Feb 1942 HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) heard heavy depth charging between 0719 B/13 and 0956 B/13. it was thought this was HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) that was being depth charged but in fact the victem of the depth charging was HMS Tempest (Lt.Cdr. W.A.K.N. Cavaye, RN) that was being hunted and forced to the surface by the Italians. (38)

14 Feb 1942 At 2004 B/14, in position 39°54'N, 17°26'E, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) picked up HE (200 revs) from most likely a motor torpedo boat on bearing 230°. Nothing was seen though but Unique dived to 60 feet. The HE moved slowly left and faded at 2235 B/14 on bearing 205° in the direction of Alice Point. (38)

15 Feb 1942 At 1310 B/15, while in position 39°56'N, 17°21'E, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) received Capt. S.10's signal timed 1110 B/15, ordering her to return to Malta through positions 39°05'N, 17°25'E and 37°30'N, 16°40'E. Course was set to comply with this signal. (40)

16 Feb 1942 At 1210 B/16, while in position 38°14'N, 17°06'E, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) received Capt. S.10's signal timed 1016 B/16, ordering her to patrol in position 38°10'N, 17°10'E until dusk to intercept enemy cruisers and destroyers returning to Taranto. Unique arrived in the position ordered at 1500 B/16. It was also stated that one of the cruisers may be damaged.

This signal must have referred to one of two Italian forces at sea at that moment. The force in question here was made up of the 7th Cruiser Division, light cruisers Emanuele Filiberto Duca d’Aosta and Raimondo Montecuccoli and the 11th Destroyer Division made up of the Aviere, Geniere, Camicia Nera and Ascari.

The two cruisers had sailed from Taranto with the battleship Caio Duilio, screened by the 13th Destroyer Division made up of the Alpino, Carabiniere, Fuciliere and Bersagliere, at 1830-1946 B/14 to intercept an enemy convoy. Duilio returned to Taranto at 2250 B/14.

The Third Cruiser Division, made up of the heavy cruisers Trento and Gorizia, screened by the 11th Destroyer Division (composition see above), had sailed from Messina at 2205-2253 B/14 and joined the 7th Cruiser Division at sea. The destroyer Alpino returned to Augusta at 1450 B/15. The 7th Cruiser Division exchanged screen with that of the Third Division and now screened by the 11th Destroyer Division returned to Taranto at 1743-2130 B/16. At 1315 B/16, the destroyer Carabiniere (now with the Third Cruiser Division) was damaged by a torpedo from HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN). The Third Cruiser Division, without escort, returned to Messina at 1520-1600 B/16.

Carabiniere towed by the torpedo boat Giuseppe Dezza, which had sailed from Augusta to assist her, then joined by the torpedo boat Generale Marcello Prestinari and the tug Instacabile which took over the tow. Carabiniere, screened by the destroyers Bersagliere and Alpino and two MAS boats, finally reached Messina at 0915 B/17.

Between 1100 B/16 and 1145 B/16 distant depth charging was heard. From 1317 B/16, constant depth charging was heard to the south-westward. This continued until around 1800 B/16. During this period no less 188 explosions were counted. This depth charging was directed at HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN).
At 1955 B/16, HMS Unique surfaced in position 38°08'N, 17°08'E and the left the area to continue her passage to Malta. (38)

18 Feb 1942 Around 1130 B/18, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) ended her 20th war patrol (18th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. This was another frustrating patrol patrol with several sightings but no attack carried out.
At 0544 B/18, while approaching Malta, HMS Unique grounded in position 089° - Rinella south-west high W/T mast - 1500 yards. She was refloated after a few hours with the assistance of HM Tug Robust.
At 1630 B/18, HMS Unique was docked in No.1 graving dock at the Malta Dockyard for inspection of the damage which was found to be moderate. (38)
27 Feb 1942 A court of inquiry was held at Malta to investigate the grounding of HMS Unique of 18 February.
28 Feb 1942 HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) is undocked and carried out diving trials off Malta. (41)

4 Mar 1942 Around 2010 B/4, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) departed Malta for Gibraltar. Unique was to proceed to the U.K. to refit. On board was an Italian human torpedo that was to be brought to the U.K. for study. Also she had only two MK. II torpedoes in the tubes and carried no reloads.

5 Mar 1942 At 0123 B/5, only a few hours after departure from Malta, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) was machine-gunned by a German Junkers 88 aircraft and forced to dive but she suffered no damage.

Went having passed the Italian declared mine area to the south and south-west of Sicily she was first to proceed to position 38°10'N, 09°00'E at 0100 B/8 and then through positions; (A) 39°00'N, 05°00'E at 2300 A/9. (B) 38°22'N, 01°42'E at 0800 A/11. (C) 36°33'N, 02°10'W at 0800 A/13. (D) 36°20'N, 04°42'W at 1000 A/14 to arrive at Gibraltar at 1500 A/14. (42)

14 Mar 1942 Around 1525 A/14, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) arrived at Gibraltar from Malta. The passage had been uneventful except for the machine gunning by a German aircraft early on the 5th. (42)
19 Mar 1942 Around 1400 A/19, HMS Unique (Lt.Cdr. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) departed Gibraltar for Portsmouth. (42)

28 Mar 1942 Around 1100 A/28, in position 180° - Lizard Point - 5 miles, HMS Unique (Lt.Cdr. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) made rendezvous with the armed yacht HMS Star of India (Lt.Cdr.(Retd.) D.M. Marshall, RN). (42)
29 Mar 1942 Around 1030 A/29, HMS Unique (Lt.Cdr. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) arrived at Portsmouth (Fort Blockhouse, Haslar Creek, Gosport) from Gibraltar. (42)
31 Mar 1942 HMS Unique (Lt.Cdr. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) departed Portsmouth for Sheerness.
Before departure the submarine was wiped (degaussed).

On departure from Portsmouth she took passage in convoy CE 73 [no further details on this convoy available for the moment]. Early the next morning the convoy scattered due to thick fog. HMS Unique anchored of Dungeness for a few hours before continuing the passage. (42)
1 Apr 1942 Around 1545 A/1, HMS Unique (Lt.Cdr. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) arrived at Sheerness from Portsmouth. (43)
2 Apr 1942 After de-ammunitioning, HMS Unique (Lt.Cdr. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN) proceeded from Sheerness to the Chatham Dockyard where she was to refit. (43)
22 Jul 1942 With her refit completed, HMS Unique (Lt. R.E. Boddington, RN) proceeded from the Chatham Dockyard to Sheerness. (44)
25 Jul 1942 HMS Unique (Lt. R.E. Boddington, RN) departed Sheerness for Portsmouth with convoy CW 110. [No further details available for the moment.] (44)
26 Jul 1942 Around 1715 B/26, HMS Unique (Lt. R.E. Boddington, RN) arrived at Portsmouth (Fort Blockhouse, Haslar Creek, Gosport) from Sheerness. (44)
27 Jul 1942 HMS Unique (Lt. R.E. Boddington, RN) conducted exercises off Portsmouth. (44)
29 Jul 1942 HMS Unique (Lt. R.E. Boddington, RN) conducted exercises off Portsmouth. (44)
30 Jul 1942 Around 0545 B/30, HMS Unique (Lt. R.E. Boddington, RN) departed Portsmouth for Plymouth.
On leaving the Solent she joined convoy PW 194. [No further details available for the moment.]
Around 2200 B/30, HMS Unique left the convoy escorted by HMS Cleveland (Cdr. G.B. Sayer, RN). (44)
31 Jul 1942 HMS Unique (Lt. R.E. Boddington, RN) and HMS Cleveland (Cdr. G.B. Sayer, RN)) arrived at Plymouth. Before entering harbour A/S exercises had been carried out.
1 Aug 1942 HMS Unique (Lt. R.E. Boddington, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Plymouth with aircraft. (45)
3 Aug 1942 HMS Unique (Lt. R.E. Boddington, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Plymouth.

Two of the ships that participated in these exercises were the escort destroyer ORP Krakowiak (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) J.A. Tchórznicki, ORP) and the ASW trawler HMS Cambridgeshire (T/Lt. H.F.B. Ashby, RNVR).
4 Aug 1942 HMS Unique (Lt. R.E. Boddington, RN) conducted exercises off Plymouth. (45)
7 Aug 1942 Around 0800 B/7, HMS Unique (Lt. R.E. Boddington, RN) departed Plymouth for Holy Loch.
She was escorted out until around noon by HMS Jasper (T/Lt. W.T. Hodson, RNVR) when HMS White Bear (Cdr.(Retd.) C.C. Flemming, RN) took over near Falmouth. (45)
9 Aug 1942 Around 1220 A/9, HMS Unique (Lt. R.E. Boddington, RN) arrived at Holy Loch from Plymouth. She was now to commence a post-refit work up period. (45)
13 Aug 1942 HMS Unique (Lt. R.E. Boddington, RN) conducted torpedo firing trials at Holy Loch. (45)
17 Aug 1942 HMS Unique (Lt. R.E. Boddington, RN) conducted exercises in the Clyde area. These included a practice attack on HMS Breda (Capt.(Retd.) A.E. Johnston, RN). (45)
18 Aug 1942 HMS Unique (Lt. R.E. Boddington, RN) and HMS Tigris (Lt.Cdr. G.R. Colvin, RN) conducted exercises in the Clyde area with HMS Breda (Capt.(Retd.) A.E. Johnston, RN).
Upon completion of these exercises HMS Unique conducted speed trials on the Arran measured mile. (45)

19 Aug 1942 HMS Unique (Lt. R.E. Boddington, RN) and HMS Tigris (Lt.Cdr. G.R. Colvin, RN) conducted exercises in the Clyde area with HMS Breda (Capt.(Retd.) A.E. Johnston, RN). Later HMS P 217 (Lt. E.J.D. Turner, DSC, RN) also joined for night exercises. (45)
20 Aug 1942 HMS Unique (Lt. R.E. Boddington, RN) returned to Holy Loch upon completion of last nights exercises. (45)
21 Aug 1942 HMS Unique (Lt. R.E. Boddington, RN) conducted sound trials in Loch Goil. (45)
22 Aug 1942 HMS Unique (Lt. R.E. Boddington, RN) conducted trials off Fairlie. (45)
25 Aug 1942 HMS Unique (Lt. R.E. Boddington, RN) proceeded from Holy Loch to Rothesay. (45)

26 Aug 1942 HMS Unique (Lt. R.E. Boddington, RN) conducted exercises in the Clyde area for the C.O.Q.C. (Commanding Officers Qualifying Course). These included practice attacks on HMS L 26 (Lt. H.D. Verschoyle, DSC, RN). (45)

27 Aug 1942 HMS Unique (Lt. R.E. Boddington, RN) conducted exercises in the Clyde area for the C.O.Q.C. (Commanding Officers Qualifying Course). These included practice attacks on a href=/allies/warships/ship/7271.html>HMS Alecto (Cdr. J.R.S. Brown, RN), HMS Breda (Capt.(Retd.) A.E. Johnston, RN), HMS Cutty Sark (Cdr.(Retd.) R.H. Mack, RN), HMS L'Incomprise (Lt.Cdr.(Retd.) F.S. Deveson, RNR) and HMS Boarhound (Skr. A. Keable, RNR). (45)

28 Aug 1942 HMS Unique (Lt. R.E. Boddington, RN) conducted attack exercises in the Clyde area with HMS Cutty Sark (Cdr.(Retd.) R.H. Mack, RN). Following these exercises HMS Unique conducted gunnery exercises. (45)
30 Aug 1942 HMS Unique (Lt. R.E. Boddington, RN) conducted compass adjustment and D/F calibration trials at Holy Loch.
[No logs of HMS Unique are available after August 1942. Some details for September / October 1942 might thus be missing.] (45)

4 Sep 1942 Around 0700 A/4, HMS Unique (Lt. R.E. Boddington, RN) and HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) departed Holy Loch for Lerwick. They were escorted by HMS Cutty Sark (Cdr.(Retd.) R.H. Mack, RN). (46)
6 Sep 1942 Around 0930 A/6, HMS Unique (Lt. R.E. Boddington, RN), HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) and HMS Cutty Sark (Cdr.(Retd.) R.H. Mack, RN) arrived at Lerwick from Holy Loch. (46)
7 Sep 1942 Around 1900 A/7, HMS Unique (Lt. R.E. Boddington, RN) departed Lerwick for her 21st war patrol. She was ordered to provide cover for convoy operations to and from Northern Russia.

19 Sep 1942 At 1323 A/19, HMS Unique (Lt. R.E. Boddington, RN) was in position 73°02'N, 18°11'E when a U-boat was sighted at a distance of six nautical miles. Both submarines submerged but contact was lost. [Neither a German U-boat or another Allied submarine reported a contact around this time.]

1323 A/19 - Sighted a U-boat bearing 310° at a range of 6 miles. Enemy course was 210°. Dived. It is considered that sighting was simultaneous as the U-boat disappeared just before HMS Unique dived. It's position was closed at high speed submerged on a course of 260°, until the estimated range was 4000 yards when the quietest speed was used.
1500 A/19 - Altered course to 210° in the hope that the enemy would lose patience and surface. Nothing more was seen or heard.
1620 A/19 - Abandoned the hunt and cleared the area remaining dived.
1834 A/19 - Surfaced. (5)
20 Sep 1942 At 0001 A/20, HMS Unique (Lt. R.E. Boddington, RN) left patrol to return to Lerwick. (5)
24 Sep 1942 Around 0800 A/24, HMS Unique (Lt. R.E. Boddington, RN) ended her 21st war patrol at Lerwick. She departed Lerwick for Holy Loch about an hour later.

She made the passage to Holy Loch together with HMS P 221 (Lt. M.F.R. Ainslie, DSC, RN), HMS HMS P 217 (Lt. E.J.D. Turner, DSC, RN) and HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN). They were escorted by HMS White Bear (Cdr. (Retd.) C.C. Flemming, RN). (46)
26 Sep 1942 Around 1230 A/26, HMS Unique (Lt. R.E. Boddington, RN) arrived at Holy Loch from Lerwick. (46)
8 Oct 1942 Around 1700 A/8, HMS Unique (Lt. R.E. Boddington, RN) departed Holy Loch for her 22nd war patrol.

Passage south through the Irish Sea was made together with HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN), HMS Sealion (Lt. D. Lambert, DSC, RN) and HMS Graph (Lt. P.B. Marriott, RN). They were escorted by HMS La Capricieuse (Lt.Cdr. G.W. Dobson, RNR).

Around 1930 A/11, when west of Wolf Rock the escort parted company and the submarines proceeded independently. Heavy weather had encountered during the passage south causing delays and submarines loosing contact with the escort momentarily.

Unique was ordered to patrol off northern Spain to intercept the German blockade runner Belgrano and to proceed to Gibraltar upon completion of this patrol where she was to join the Mediterranean Fleet. HMS Unique was allocated patrol zone K 502 with 44°40'N as northern limit, 44°10'N as southern limit, 05°30'W as eastern limit and 07°00'W as western limit. She was to proceed to her patrol area (E.T.A. 0530 A/14) through positions (B) 48°00'N, 06°30'W, (D) 47°28'N, 06°31'W, (E) 45°35'N, 03°42'W and (G) 45°05'N, 03°31'W. (48)

13 Oct 1942 At 0845 A/13 (0945 B/13 = German time), the German tanker Spichern (9323 GRT, built 1935, code name 'Elm', former Norwegian Krossfonn captured on 26 June 1940 by raider Widder) reported that a loud explosion was heard followed by a large column of water about 2000 meters away to port. From the same direction a torpedo track was seen. The Commanding Officer ordered hard to port and the torpedo was observed to run on a parallel course and miss about 50 metres from the port side. Fire was opened with 10.5 cm and 3.7 cm guns. Position of the attack was 46°54'N, 06°03'W.

At 0855 A/13 (0955 B/13 = German time), a loud noise similar to a depth charge was heard and two more torpedo tracks were observed at about 3000 metres astern on the port side. They missed the port side by 70 metres. Position of these attacks as reported by Spichern was 46°54'N, 06°03'W.

Spichern was on her way back to France after she had been damaged by Allied aircraft while attempting to pass the Bay of Biscay for operations in the Atlantic on 12 October 1942. Shortly after these submarines attacks (at 1045 hours), her escort to St. Nazaire, the German minesweepers M 24, M 32 and M 152 joined her. Later (at 2345 hours) Sperrbrecher 175 joined for the last part of the passage into St. Nazaire. Spichern anchored in St. Nazaire at 0031/14.

At 0852 A/13 and 0854 A/13, HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN), while in position 46°32'N, 05°55'W, heard two loud explosions. This position was about 23 nautical miles from the position reported by Spichern.

The torpedo attack on the Spichern was not reported by any other submarine on patrol at that moment. The attacker must have been HMS Unique (Lt. R.E. Boddington, RN). No enemy report was received from Unique. It therefore is possible that HMS Unique was lost during the attack on Spichern. What is reported by Spichern looks like two attacks with two torpedoes each. It seems like that during the first attack one of the torpedoes fired prematured possibly damaging Unique. It seems likely Unique was able to fire another two torpedoes at the Spichern. Possibly the second explosion heard by Spichern was the demise of HMS Unique (Lt. R.E. Boddington, RN).
HMS Ursula also reported hearing faint rumbling noises between 1100 A/13 and 1300 A/13. (49)

18 Oct 1942 In a signal timed 0021 A/18, F.O.S. (Flag Officer Submarines) ordered HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN) and HMS Unique (Lt. R.E. Boddington, RN) to leave patrol forthwith on the surface for Gibraltar routed as follows: HMS Ursula through positions 44°40'N, 09°59'W and 43°02'N, 11°15'W. HMS Unique through positions 44°10'N, 09°01'W and 43°01'N, 10° 30'W. thence both as ordered by F.O.C.N.A. (Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic).
HMS P 54 (Lt. C.E. Oxborrow, DSC, RN) proceed forthwith on surface through positions 47°01'N, 12° 01'E and 43°01'N, 12°10'W thence as ordered by F.O.C.N.A. (50)

22 Oct 1942 In a signal timed 1936 A/22, F.O.C.N.A. (Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic), ordered HMS Unique (Lt. R.E. Boddington, RN) to reported her E.T.A. time at Gibraltar. There was no reply.
In a signal timed 2006 A/22, F.O.C.N.A. ordered HMS Unique to report her position, course and speed. There was no reply. (50)
